entry.title: SAP SuccessFactors 1H 2026: The Skills-Based Organisation Finally Grows Up
entry.content:
Two years ago, when I wrote about the 1H 2024 release of the Talent Intelligence Hub, I described TIH as the foundation of a skills-based organisation. At that point, much of the conversation was still aspirational — organisations were asking whether skills belonged at the centre of their HR strategy, and how the SuccessFactors suite would connect jobs, skills and people in one consistent model.

The 1H 2026 release is a different kind of moment. This is not a release of bright, shiny new toys. This is a maturation release — the point at which  SAP SuccessFactors is telling customers, very clearly, that the skills-based organisation is no longer a strategic aspiration. It is now the operational architecture of the suite, and the release makes three things unmistakable:

  1. Job Profile Builder (JPB) plus the Talent Intelligence Hub (TIH) is the future. Job Description Manager is being retired.
  2. Governance, sources of truth and trust have become first-class citizens. Skills data is finally being treated with the discipline HR data has always deserved.
  3. Career mobility, development and succession are being re-platformed on top of this foundation, with Joule agents doing some of the heaviest lifting.

If you are running SuccessFactors at enterprise scale — and especially if you have been putting off hard conversations about your job architecture or your skills governance — this release is going to force those conversations. In this blog, I'll walk through the updates that genuinely matter for leaders working on skills, job architecture, and career & talent development.

Let's get into it.

Part 1 — Job Architecture Finally Reflects Reality

For years, one of the most common blockers I have seen in real-world implementations has been the two-level job architecture in Job Profile Builder: Family → Role. It was a clean model on paper, but it did not survive contact with most large organisations. Engineering, Sales, Operations and Clinical functions rarely sit in a single flat family. They have sub-disciplines, specialisations, career streams, and technical ladders that matter enormously for skills mapping, learning and mobility.

Multi-Level Job Architecture in Job Profile Builder

This is, in my view, the headline feature of the entire 1H 2026 release on the skills side.

Customers can now configure up to four sub-family levels between Family and Role, giving a maximum of six levels in the hierarchy. Attributes — skills, competencies, and custom attributes — can be assigned at any level, and they cascade down through the hierarchy to the role, and from the role into the employee's Growth Portfolio and every downstream module that depends on the role (Performance, Career, Succession, Recruiting).

The nuance I want to highlight is that this cascading behaviour applies even if you are not using multiple levels yet. If you only have Family and Role today, you can now assign attributes at the family level and they will flow to the role and into the Growth Portfolio. That single change alone simplifies governance enormously for organisations with large, well-structured role catalogues.

At role level you retain full override flexibility — you can change proficiencies or replace inherited skills without affecting the parent level.

Strategic implication: If you have previously made painful architectural compromises to fit your organisation into the two-level model — flattening sub-disciplines, duplicating roles across families, or pushing complexity into custom fields — now is the time to revisit your design. Multi-level architecture is not just a technical upgrade; it is an opportunity to rebuild your job catalogue in a way that reflects how your business actually operates, and to do so without losing the inheritance benefits that make maintenance sustainable.

Self-Service Upgrade to JPB via Upgrade Center

Another quiet-but-important change: customers can now trigger the upgrade to Job Profile Builder directly from Admin Center → Upgrade Center, rather than raising a provisioning request through SAP Professional Services or an implementation partner.

This lowers the barrier to entry significantly. For customers who have been sitting on JDM simply because the upgrade felt bureaucratic, that excuse is now gone. (A friendly warning: if you see "Job Profile Builder" under Important Upgrades, it means you are only partially upgraded today — the old JDM-to-JPB technical sync job and the JPB enablement are now consolidated into a single action, and completing this is a prerequisite for the multi-level architecture feature.)

Migration of Role Descriptions from JDM to JPB

Historically, one of the real frictions in upgrading from JDM was that role descriptions did not migrate cleanly — customers had to export, re-format and re-import content into the new job profile structure. With 1H 2026, JDM role descriptions are automatically converted into job profiles in JPB during the upgrade. This removes a real practical barrier and should accelerate downstream adoption of Recruiting, Performance and Career processes that depend on JPB.

The Big One: JDM Retirement by 2H 2027

SAP has now formally announced the sunset of Job Description Manager by 2H 2027. This is not a surprise — development on JDM effectively stopped some years ago, and JPB has been the strategic successor for five-plus years — but the formal retirement date changes the conversation.

If you are still on JDM, you now have a clear, time-bound roadmap decision to make. My advice:

  • Treat this as a strategic opportunity, not a technical migration. Most organisations I speak with carry five to ten years of accumulated job-catalogue technical debt. Use the move to JPB as the forcing function to clean it up.
  • Plan for the Talent Intelligence Hub at the same time. JPB is a prerequisite for TIH, and TIH unlocks AI-driven experiences (skills inferencing, career intelligence, Joule agents) that will increasingly differentiate employee experience. Doing these sequentially often doubles the change-management effort; doing them together lets you tell one coherent story to the business.
  • Do not wait until 2027. The teams who wait will be competing for the same scarce SAP Professional Services and partner capacity in the final months before the cutoff.

JPB will remain the admin interface where you define families, roles and the organisational job structure. TIH is the repository for person-related attributes (skills, competencies, certificates). The two live side-by-side and depend on each other.

Part 2 — TIH Matures: Certificates, Governance, and Trust

The second major theme of 1H 2026 is that the Talent Intelligence Hub is growing up. Skills data is finally being treated with the same rigour we have always applied to core HR data — with proper sources of truth, governance, and access control.

Certificates as a First-Class Attribute in TIH

Certificate Administration arrives as a new attribute type in TIH, with a full lifecycle: administrators can define certificates, attach documents and credential metadata, set validity periods and renewal requirements, and — crucially — link certificates to skills and required proficiency levels.

From the employee side, adding a certificate to the Growth Portfolio automatically grants the associated skills at the configured proficiency levels. If the employee already holds the skill, the certificate appears in the skill's timeline. Expiry reminders are triggered automatically at 90/60/30/7 days ahead of expiry, and managers get a centralised Team View of certificate status across direct reports.

One thoughtful design choice worth flagging: when a certificate expires or is deleted, the associated skills are retained. This matches the existing Growth Portfolio behaviour (skills persist even if a role mapping changes) and avoids the pathological outcome of employees losing capability ratings overnight because a training record lapsed.

Where this matters most: regulated industries. Manufacturing, pharmaceutical, utilities, financial services, aviation, healthcare — any sector where compliance audits depend on demonstrating that staff hold current credentials — now has a native, skills-linked, auditable system of record inside SuccessFactors. This is a big deal. For my manufacturing and life-sciences clients, this single feature changes the ROI calculation on TIH.

A couple of items I would watch on the roadmap: native integration with LMS curricula and background elements, and the ability to associate certificates as role attributes in Job Profile Builder. SAP has confirmed both are in development but not yet delivered.

 

 

Skills Governance Goes Mainstream (with AI)

From 1H 2026, Skills Governance is enabled by default in all instances and sits within Manage Talent Intelligence Hub. If you have AI-assisted skills inferencing enabled, newly inferred skills flow into an Inferred tab where administrators can review, standardise, or reject them before they land in the official skills library. Imported skills can optionally be subjected to the same governance flow.

The enriched Admin UI consolidates attribute management, settings, tags, rating scales and rating history into a structured workspace — a significant improvement for skills architects who previously had to navigate across several screens.

 

My view: this is the single most important governance capability TIH has ever shipped. One of the consistent failure modes I have seen in skills programmes is the rapid proliferation of near-duplicate, inconsistently named skills from multiple sources (performance feedback, CVs, third-party inference engines, manual entry). Without governance, the skills library becomes noisy within months and trust collapses. AI-assisted standardisation, inside the suite, is how you keep the library clean at enterprise scale.

Manage Data Sources & Manage Growth Portfolio Ratings

These two features are twin pillars of the same idea: controlled, auditable, trusted skills data.

  • Manage Data Sources lets administrators register external skills providers (Beamery, Degreed, TechWolf, TalenTeam, and others) as verified sources feeding into TIH and the Growth Portfolio. You can assign a custom display name so that what appears in the employee's timeline matches how your organisation describes the process (e.g., "Career Pulse" rather than the vendor's product name).
  • Manage Growth Portfolio Ratings lets you decide, per internal and external source, whether that source is allowed to overwrite proficiency levels, or only to raise them. It is now possible to say: "Our Performance Management forms can overwrite proficiency levels, but our skills inference engine can only add signals and increase ratings — never lower them."

If you are currently integrating external skills data into Growth Portfolio via Inbound APIs, note that this is no longer optional: you must register your sources and assign the new Import/Export Talent Intelligence Hub and Growth Portfolio Data role-based permission, otherwise data flow will break. Plan this as part of your 1H 2026 release testing.

Restricting Attributes by Type or Tag

A governance feature customers have been asking for: you can now configure target criteria in RBP to restrict which attribute types (skills, competencies, custom) or tags a particular user or group can see and act on across Growth Portfolio, Opportunity Marketplace, People Profile, Job Profile Builder, Succession, Performance Management and more. It is optional, with specific supported and excluded scenarios documented — but for organisations juggling compliance constraints across geographies and business units, this is the granularity that has been missing.

Part 3 — Employee and Manager Experience

Skill-Based Search in People Search

With Growth Portfolio enabled, users can now search for employees by skills, attributes and proficiency levels directly from the enhanced People Search experience. For internal mobility, project staffing, and rapid expertise discovery, this is a long-overdue capability. You will need to manually enable Enhanced People Search in Admin Center and ensure the Skill and Attribute search criteria is selected — this is not automatic on upgrade.

Growth Portfolio on Mobile

Growth Portfolio is now available on the SuccessFactors mobile app (iOS and Android). This is a first-phase release focused on employee self-service — adding and managing attributes on the go. The web experience remains richer, but the mobile capability will materially change adoption curves, especially for frontline, field and deskless workforces. For my manufacturing and retail clients, this is the difference between a skills programme that reaches head office and one that reaches the whole business.

Growth Portfolio Data Export Enhancements

Finally — and this will make integration architects very happy — you can now filter and sort Growth Portfolio data in Integration Center using the new Filter and Sort tab, including by personUuid, libraryId, proficiency level, status and last modified date. Combined with the new dedicated import/export RBP, this gives much tighter control over what flows in and out of the skills repository.

Part 4 — Career & Talent Development: Where the Skills Story Pays Off

Everything in Parts 1 to 3 is foundation. Part 4 is where the foundation starts paying dividends for employees, managers, and talent leaders.

Show Upcoming Role for Internal Hires

When an employee has accepted an internal move, their upcoming role now appears in the Growth Portfolio alongside their current role — with role details, skill gaps, and development actions. This removes the awkward gap between offer acceptance and effective start date, where an internal hire has historically had no way to start preparing.

Enhanced Team Development Insights

Managers can now see, for each direct report: detailed role information, skills and proficiency levels, and the skills each team member has marked as a passion. This last point — surfacing skill passions at the team level — is small in engineering terms but large in coaching terms. It gives managers something more human to discuss than a compliance-driven development conversation.

Enhanced Personalised Recommendations

This is one of the quieter but more important upgrades. Recommendations for roles, open jobs, and learning are now materially smarter:

  • Roles: recommendations can now filter out roles below the employee's current level, using a number-type custom field. No more demotion-shaped career paths.
  • Learning: items linked to more relevant skills appear first, and items below the employee's current proficiency level are excluded. This raises the quality bar considerably.
  • Open Jobs: administrators can configure preferred locations and appropriate role levels as filter criteria. Mismatched jobs are simply not recommended rather than shown as noise.

Taken together, these changes move the recommendation engine from "broadly relevant" to "genuinely personalised" — which is what it takes for employees to actually trust the system enough to act on it.

Invite Users to Apply for Assignments

Assignment owners and co-owners can now proactively identify candidates and invite them to apply to Opportunity Marketplace assignments. Invited users receive an email, a home-page invitation card (if the latest home page is enabled), and see the assignment surfaced at the top of their Opportunity Marketplace and Growth Portfolio recommendations. This closes the loop on internal mobility — not just discovery, but active outreach.

Custom Attributes in Talent Search

Talent Search can now use custom attributes alongside skills and competencies, including side-by-side proficiency comparison and export of attribute data. For organisations that have invested in custom attributes to capture regulatory certifications, clearances, language fluency, or industry-specific capabilities, this unlocks a much richer internal talent discovery experience.

Part 5 — The Joule Agents for Career & Talent Development

1H 2026 introduces three Joule agents directly relevant to the skills and talent development agenda.

Career Development Agent

The Career Development Agent guides employees through their career journey conversationally inside Joule. It evaluates strengths, identifies viable next roles grounded in skills data, and — here is the part that matters — can automatically assemble a personalised development plan with monthly milestones, recommended learning, recommended mentors, and concrete activities. It blends traditional role progressions with skills-adjacent opportunities, which matters for organisations trying to move beyond linear career ladders.

Development Goal Creation Agent

This agent generates personalised, measurable development goals grounded in CPM feedback, role data, and skills insights. Instead of vague competency-based goals ("improve leadership"), it produces impact statements with timelines ("Enhance cost control and budget management skills to improve financial oversight — 6-month time frame"). It also surfaces gaps, duplicates, and missing metrics across existing goals — which, in practice, is probably where most of the real hygiene value sits.

Succession Planning Agent

For succession planners, this agent performs a succession health check — identifying critical positions with no named successors, positions with low bench strength, and organisation-wide risk patterns. It then provides prioritised action plans. For large enterprises with hundreds of critical positions, this compresses days of manual analysis into minutes.

A pragmatic reminder on licensing. Joule agents require a Joule Premium license plus AI Units for consumption-based pricing. This is something to scope and plan with your account team early — the business case for agents is strong, but it is not a free upgrade. Treat it the way you would any consumption-based cloud service: model your usage, set guardrails, and tie adoption to measurable value.

What Leaders Should Actually Do Now

Rather than end with a feature summary, let me end with the conversations I think you should have in the next quarter:

  1. Book the JDM-to-JPB conversation on your 2026 roadmap. The 2H 2027 sunset is non-negotiable, and the firms that plan early will migrate with more control, cleaner data, and less partner capacity risk.
  2. Revisit your job architecture. Multi-level capability changes what good looks like. If your current model was a compromise, now is the moment to correct it — and to let cascading attributes do work that job-code gymnastics used to do.
  3. Stand up skills governance now. Skills Governance is on by default from 1H 2026. Assign a skills data steward. Define your policy for inferred, imported and standardised skills. The library only stays trustworthy if someone owns it.
  4. Register your external skills sources and tighten your import/export permissions. If you are integrating Beamery, Degreed, TechWolf, TalenTeam or any other partner, you will need to register and configure permissions to keep data flowing. Plan this as part of release testing.
  5. Make certificates part of your compliance story. If you operate in a regulated industry, this is the release where SuccessFactors genuinely becomes a compliance system of record for workforce credentials. Build the business case around audit readiness, not just HR efficiency.
  6. Design your Joule agent rollout deliberately. Start with the agent that matches your highest-pain use case — Succession Planning Agent for large complex organisations, Career Development Agent for organisations trying to drive internal mobility, Development Goal Creation Agent for organisations with goal-quality issues. Measure before you scale.

Closing Thoughts

I have written before about mastering the implementation of SAP SuccessFactors Talent Intelligence Hub, about building the business case for skills, and about where organisations sit on the skills maturity curve. Across all of those pieces, one theme recurs: skills transformation is not a product implementation — it is a business transformation enabled by a product.

1H 2026 is the release where SAP SuccessFactors' product finally gives you most of what you need to run that transformation at scale. Multi-level architecture, skills governance, source-of-truth controls, certificates, mobile, consent-based succession, and the Career & Talent Development Joule agents together form a genuinely coherent platform.

The hard work — job architecture design, skills governance operating model, change management, adoption, business-case tracking — is still on you and your teams. But the tooling has caught up to the ambition.

If your organisation is starting this journey, or if you have hit a wall with an existing TIH or skills programme, I am always happy to compare notes. Reach out in the comments, or find us at talenteam.com.

And if you are joining us at our Learning & Skills Forum in Philadelphia, Chicago or London this year, this release — and what it means for the future of the skills-based organisation — will be front and centre.

entry.title: High-Availability Setup for SAP Secure Login Service with AWS Route 53
entry.content:
Introduction

Inspired by the initial blog by our Product team members

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/high-availability-setup-for-sap-secure-login-service-with-microsoft-azure/ba-p/14140475

SAP Secure Login Service for SAP GUI enables secure authentication and single sign-on (SSO) for SAP GUI using SAP BTP, leveraging its built-in platform capabilities for high availability 

In most cases, this is sufficient, as the service is only used during the user’s initial login. Once authenticated, an X.509 certificate provides SSO for the rest of the session.

However, some organizations require higher resilience and opt for a multi-region failover setup. In this blog, I’ll walk through how to implement this using SAP Secure Login Service, SAP BTP Custom Domain Service, and AWS Route 53.

Architecture 

Prerequisites

SAP BTP

  • Two subaccounts in different regions (e.g. US10, US21, EU10 etc). For this blog i have used US10 and EU10.
  • SLS subscribed in both subaccounts
  • Custom Domain Service entitlement assigned and activated in both subaccounts

AWS

  • Route 53 hosted zone for your domain

Identity

  • IAS tenants configured with SLS

Certificates

  • TLS certificate for sls.yourdomain.com (from a public CA)

Step 1: Configure Custom Domain Service — US10 (Primary)

In the US10 subaccount, open Custom Domain Manager and complete the following:

Reserve domain: yourdomain.com

Create custom domain: sls.yourdomain.com

 

Create a SaaS route: map sls.yourdomain.com to the SLS subscription

 

Perform TLS configurations

Create a Server Identity and generate a CSR (Certificate Signing Request)

Obtain a TLS certificate from a public CA using the CSR and Upload the full certificate chain and activate it

Activate it

Note the CNAME target shown under "DNS CNAME Recommendation" — you will need this exact value for Route 53

About the CNAME target
The CNAME target provided by Custom Domain Manager is SAP's ingress hostname for SaaS routes in the US10 region. Use exactly what Custom Domain Manager shows. Do not substitute or guess any hostname.

Step 2: Configure Custom Domain Service — EU10 (Secondary)

Repeat exactly the same steps in the EU10 subaccount. Use the same custom domain: sls.yourdomain.com.

Note the CNAME target for EU10 as well.

Why the same hostname in both subaccounts?
Each subaccount independently maps sls.yourdomain.com to its local SLS SaaS instance. This is fully supported by Custom Domain Service. Both instances respond to the same hostname, which is what makes Route 53 failover work transparently. Route 53 resolves sls.yourdomain.com to the CNAME target of whichever region is active — and that region's SLS instance is already configured to serve requests for that hostname.

Step 3: Configure IAS Redirect URI

In your IAS tenants, add the following as an allowed redirect URI for the SLS application:

https://sls.yourdomain.com/login/callback

 

Keep all existing redirect URIs — just add this one. Because both SLS instances share the same hostname, this single redirect URI covers both regions regardless of which instance handles the authentication request.

 Each SLS instance uses a separate IAS tenant, add this redirect URI in both tenants ( US10 and EU10)

Step 4: Configure Route 53 Failover Routing

Health Checks

Create two Route 53 health checks — one per SLS instance — targeting each SLS instance hostname directly:

Health Check 1 (Primary — US10):

  • Protocol: HTTPS
  • Endpoint: <your-us10-sls-hostname> (the actual SLS instance hostname in US10, e.g. yourinstance.us10.sls.cloud.sap)
  • Path: /health
  • Port: 443
  • Purpose: Monitors US10 SLS instance directly

Health Check 2 (Secondary — EU10):

  • Protocol: HTTPS
  • Endpoint: <your-eu10-sls-hostname> (the actual SLS instance hostname in EU10, e.g. yourinstance.eu10.sls.cloud.sap)
  • Path: /health
  • Port: 443
  • Purpose: Monitors EU10 SLS instance directly

(like primary US10 setup)

Why target the SLS instance hostname directly?
The health checks must target each SLS instance hostname directly — not sls.yourdomain.com. At any point in time, sls.yourdomain.com only resolves to the currently active region. A health check on sls.yourdomain.com would only monitor whichever instance Route 53 is currently routing to, and would never detect that the primary is down (because Route 53 would have already switched). By monitoring each SLS instance hostname independently, Route 53 can detect failures in either region at any time.

CNAME records for failover

Primary Record

Name:            sls.yourdomain.com

Type:            CNAME

Value:           api.cf.us10.hana.ondemand.com

Routing policy:  Failover — PRIMARY

Health check:    Yes (associate Health Check 1 — US10)

 

 

Secondary Record

Similarly for Secondary

 

TTL recommendation
Could set TTL to 60 seconds. A lower TTL means faster DNS propagation during failover events.

Step 5: Configure SAP Secure Login Client

Configure SAP Secure Login Client (SLC) with the SLS URL pointing to your custom domain:

https://sls.yourdomain.com/slc/v1/login

 

 

Distribute this configuration via:

  • GPO (Group Policy Object)
  • Intune
  • SCCM

Because the URL always points to sls.yourdomain.com, no SLC reconfiguration is needed when a failover occurs. Route 53 changes the DNS resolution transparently.

Failover Behavior

Scenario

Result

Primary healthy

Traffic → US10 SLS SaaS instance

Primary fails health check

Route 53 switches DNS to EU10 CNAME target

Primary recovers

Route 53 switches traffic back to US10

Failover Timing

  • Health check detection: ~30–90 seconds
  • DNS TTL propagation: ~60 seconds
  • Total worst-case failover time: approximately 2 minutes
 

 

Important Considerations

  1. Failover Is NOT Session-Aware
    Active login flows that are in progress during a failover event may fail. Users may need to retry authentication. Design user communications accordingly and set expectations with support teams.
  2. SLS Instances Are Independent
    Both SLS instances must be kept in sync manually. This includes IAS configuration, authentication policies, certificate trust configuration, and version levels. Drift between instances can cause inconsistent behavior depending on which region is active.
  3. Certificate Strategy
    You can use the same TLS certificate (same Subject Alternative Name) across both regions for simplicity, since both Custom Domain Manager instances are serving the same hostname. Alternatively, use separate certificates per region for better security isolation. Either approach is valid.
  4. Health Checks on Both Records
    It is recommended to add health checks on both primary and secondary records. Without a health check on the secondary, Route 53 may route traffic to a failed secondary during a primary failover, resulting in an outage even though the primary was available.

Testing

1. DNS Verification

nslookup sls.yourdomain.com

Confirm it resolves to the US10 CNAME target under normal conditions.

2. End-to-End Login

Open SAP GUI Secure Login Client and authenticate end-to-end. Confirm the full OIDC flow completes, including the redirect back to sls.yourdomain.com/login/callback.

3. Failover Simulation

Disable the primary health check in Route 53. Wait approximately 2 minutes, then run:

nslookup sls.yourdomain.com

Confirm it now resolves to the EU10 CNAME target.

4. Secondary Authentication

Verify that authentication still works end-to-end when Route 53 is routing to the EU10 instance.

5. Failback

Re-enable the primary health check. After the TTL expires, confirm DNS resolves back to the US10 CNAME target and authentication continues to work.

SAP Backend user mapping

There are two options to ensure that the SNC mapping remains intact:

  • Use parameters of the SAP Cryptographic Library to strip the region names from the certificate subject before the mapping takes place. That way, certificates from different regions will match the same SNC name. See note 2338952 for details.
  • Change the SAP Secure Login Service configuration to use a customer-managed certificate authority. This gives you the option to customize the subject name of the certificate so that it does not include the BTP region.

Conclusion

By combining SAP BTP Custom Domain Service and AWS Route 53 failover routing, you eliminate the single point of failure in SAP GUI authentication. The key enabler is the shared custom domain: both SLS SaaS instances respond to sls.yourdomain.com, making the OIDC redirect_uri consistent across regions regardless of which instance is currently active.

Route 53 health checks detect failures automatically and update DNS within approximately two minutes. End users and SAP Secure Login Client configurations require no changes when a failover or failback occurs.

Final Takeaway
This setup provides:

  • High availability for SAP GUI authentication via a managed SaaS architecture
  • Transparent DNS-level failover with no client reconfiguration
  • A consistent OIDC redirect URI across both regions

 

SAP Secure Login Service for SAP GUI @Christian_Cohrs1

entry.title: Re: Total Compensation Plan Template
entry.content: Hello @CSmith: One other question. Can we use different number formats for Variable Pay and Compensation separately? Our requirement is to use money and amounts to be nearest dollar for Variable Pay but with 2 decimals on the Compensation. Is this possible to achieve?

entry.title: Re: Problem reinstalling SAP ASE
entry.content:
Can you upload the *.log file(s) from the /opt/sap/ASE-16_0/install directory?

Of particular interest is the dataserver errorlog file (REINCLOUD.log), but any other *.log files may be of use if it turns out the issue is not related to the core ASE installation.

Also, do not filter out any lines (eg, don't run a 'grep' to keep/remove lines).

entry.title: Re: Error while using SAP Fiori in browser
entry.content: Could you check in the backend in ST22? Or check your Developer Tools > Network tab which service / file generates this issue?

entry.title: Personas 3.0 : "dynamic image control"
entry.content:
Dear Expert,

I have a question regarding "dynamic image control" from pictures in the file server.

What I tried are as follows;
1. Create a flavor, put "Image control".
URL property is "SAPR3://63D102D8BAE51FD18FD1DA3FE284353F?version=20260422210417"
2. Change "URL property" of above by script.
--> I could specify other GUID of Resources exists  in "Personas Admin menu".

Question
We'd like to display a picture in the file server directly.
      (ex: /image/employees/xxx.png).
How could I achieve it?
1. Could I change URL to refer the file server directly? If so, could you give me an example?
2. or  Do I need to upload all images in advance by T-cd /PERSONAS/RESOUCES/?
3. or Do I need to use "HTML Viewer" instead of "Image Control"?

Thank you
Yuko

entry.title: Re: How to access secondary hierarchy in SAC excel formulas?
entry.content: I think I have found the solution. One can choose the hierarchy in the dimensions context menu .

entry.title: Re: SAP UI5: ISSUE : Calling Deep Entity of JSON - SAP UI...
entry.content:
You can try this, I am wondering if bindings like that for a v2 odata model is supported. For JSON model it is however.

<Text text="{ path: 'Customer_Model>to_BusinessPartnerAddress', formatter: '.formatter.firstAddressCity' }" />
<Text text="{ path: 'Customer_Model>to_BusinessPartnerAddress', formatter: '.formatter.firstEmail' }" />
<Text text="{ path: 'Customer_Model>to_BusinessPartnerAddress', formatter: '.formatter.firstPhone' }" />

JS Formatter:

formatter: {
    firstAddressCity(oAddresses) {
        console.log(`Addresses`, oAddresses);
        return oAddresses?.results?.[0]?.CityName ?? "";
    },

    firstEmail(oAddresses) {
        const oFirstAddress = oAddresses?.results?.[0];
        return oFirstAddress?.to_EmailAddress?.results?.[0]?.EmailAddress ?? "";
    },

    firstPhone(oAddresses) {
        const oFirstAddress = oAddresses?.results?.[0];
        return oFirstAddress?.to_MobilePhoneNumber?.results?.[0]?.PhoneNumber ?? "";
    }
}

If it still doesn't work, there is one console log. Check it and you can see how the structure is.

entry.title: Introduction: Software Engineer Exploring SAP BTP & Enterprise Integration
entry.content:
Who I am

Hi I'm Myrr, based in ATL, GA, working as a software engineer focused on building scalable web products.

How I got here

I started my career in a completely different field (environmental chemistry), and transitioning into tech showed me how much I enjoy solving complex, open-ended problems. That curiosity is what led me to explore SAP and enterprise systems more deeply.

What I’m building right now

I’m currently working through SAP BTP-related learning and certifications while also building my own product on the side, with a focus on using data in meaningful ways.

My SAP interests

I’m especially interested in SAP BTP and how modern applications integrate with enterprise systems. I’m drawn to the intersection of backend architecture, APIs, and scalable product design.

I’d love to connect with:

  • SAP BTP developers and architects
  • People working on real-world enterprise integrations

entry.title: order balance is zero, but Total Cost Variance is not zero?
entry.content:
Dear SAP ,

 

Why in app Analyze Production Costs - Event-Based, order balance is zero, but Total Cost Variance is not zero?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: order balance is zero, but Total Cost Variance is not...
entry.content:
The Order Balance typically hits zero because every cost debited to the order is offset by a credit.

The Total Cost Variance is an analytical value that measures the difference between the Actual Costs incurred and the Target Costs (standard cost based on the quantity received).

Even if the balance is zero, the variance remains non-zero.

entry.title: Re: How to dynamically determine Warehouse Source Storage...
entry.content:
Hello,

The most standard way to achieve this requirement in EWM would be to have 3 different storage types mapped to 3 different availability group. This would mean having ECC setup which creates a sales delivery from the right storage location dependent on the customer. I doubt the ECC setup is standard and would need some coding. To complete the setup, you need to define 3 stock types and the stock removal search sequence dependent on the stock types.

Alternatively, you can meet this requirement in EWM directly by putting a custom code in the BADI which uses interface  /SCWM/IF_EX_CORE_RMS_STRATEGY. I would suggest careful due diligence is done considering future implications and data maintenance.

Regards,

Sushant

entry.title: Handling Cross-App Navigation from Flat to Hierarchical Models in Fiori Elements
entry.content:
Cross-app navigation in SAP Fiori Elements (CAP, OData V4) can become tricky when the source and target applications follow different data models. 

In our case, we had a scenario where a flat source application needed to navigate to a hierarchical target application using intent-based navigation.

• Source App: Flat entity model, exposing fields from multiple levels of Target App's hierarchy in a single entity. Navigation source: List Report
• Target App: Hierarchical entity model, top-to-bottom. Navigation target: Object Page

Goal: A row selection in the Source App's List Report triggers intent-based navigation to the corresponding Object Page in the Target App.

 

Standard Setup

  • Semantic Object annotation on Source App's entity
annotate service.SourceAppEntity with @(Common: {SemanticKey: [ID]});
  •  Outbound navigation in Source App's  manifest.json
"crossNavigation": {
    "outbounds": {
        "TargetApp-manage": {
          "semanticObject": "BusinessObject",
          "action": "manage"
        }
    }
}
  • Inbound navigation in TargetApp's manifest.json
"crossNavigation": {
    "inbounds": {
        "TargetApp-manage": {
            "semanticObject": "BusinessObject",
            "action": "manage",
            "signature": {       
              "parameters": {},
              "additionalParameters": "allowed"
            }
        }
     }
}

 

The Problem

With the above configuration in place, the navigation was triggered but failed to resolve correctly to the target Object Page. Inspecting the navigation call in the browser showed that the FLP was receiving the entire binding context of the selected row as navigation parameters.

Since the 'Source App' is a flat entity, this included:

  • Fields from multiple hierarchy levels
  • Fields not present in Target App's entity

The Launchpad attempts to resolve navigation using all provided parameters. When those parameters don’t align with the target entity, resolution becomes unreliable.

The semantic key (ID), the only required parameter was effectively buried among irrelevant fields.

 

Root Cause

This is a data model mismatch:

  • Source App is intentionally broad (flat) to surface data across levels
  • Target App is narrow (hierarchical) and expects a specific key

By default, Fiori Elements passes the full row context and does not account for this mismatch.

 

Solution: @ui.ExcludeFromNavigationContext

The UI vocabulary provides an annotation specifically for this [1] : @ui.ExcludeFromNavigationContext

Applied to a property, it tells the Fiori Elements framework to exclude that field from the navigation context payload when intent-based navigation is triggered.

Apply it to every field in Source App's entity except the semantic key:

annotate service.SourceAppEntity with {
    field1  @ui.ExcludeFromNavigationContext;
    field2  @ui.ExcludeFromNavigationContext;
    field3  @ui.ExcludeFromNavigationContext;
    .
    .
     // repeat for all fields except ID
};

With this in place, the navigation context carries only the ID, which matches the Target App’s inbound signature and allows the Object Page to resolve correctly

 

Key Takeaway

When navigating from a flat source model to a hierarchical target model, this leads to unnecessary parameters being included in the navigation payload. @ui.ExcludeFromNavigationContext allows explicit control so that only the semantic key is passed to the target application.

  2. Incoming G/L Account-1021++++
  3. Outgoing G/L Account- 1022++++

As part of agreement with the client, we have decided that the last 4 digits is the last four digits of the account number in bank.

entry.title: Re: [S/4 HANA Public Cloud] eDocuments created for Peppol...
entry.content:
There are two options:

Option1 : 

Control via eDocument Type / Country Assignment (Configuration-Based)

SAP DRC determines eligibility before submission based on:

  • Company Code country
  • eDocument Type
  • Scenario / Network (PEPPOL)

What to do:

  1. Create / use country‑specific eDocument types

    • Example:
      • INV_PEPPOL_BE → Belgium
      • INV_PEPPOL_DE → Germany

  2. Assign eDocument types strictly by company code country

    • Belgium company code → BE eDocument type only
    • Germany company code → DE eDocument type only

  3. Do NOT assign PEPPOL eDocument type to cross‑border scenario

    • DE company code + BE customer → no valid PEPPOL eDocument type exists
      → eDocument is created but never submitted

Option 2:

BAdI-Based Filtering at Submission Level

If configuration alone is not enough (very common), SAP explicitly supports logic-based filtering using the eDocument framework.

Where to filter:

Using DRC technical enhancement points (country‑neutral, SAP‑recommended) [blog.sap-press.com]

What logic to apply:

Block submission when:

Company Code Country ≠ Customer Country

Example logic (conceptual):

  • DE company code
  • Customer country = BE
  • Channel = PEPPOL → Do not submit

My recommendation would be :

Primary control

  • Country‑specific PEPPOL eDocument types
  • Assigned strictly by company code country

Secondary (mandatory in real projects)

  • DRC BAdI filter:
    Allow PEPPOL only when Company Country = Customer Country

entry.title: Visualize Your Application Vulnerabilities in the BTP Cockpit
entry.content:
In December 2025, we introduced the Application Vulnerability Report (beta) for SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP), giving you an API-driven way to detect open-source vulnerabilities in your Cloud Foundry applications. Today, we are excited to announce that the service now comes with its first graphical user interface - accessible directly from the BTP Cockpit.

If you have been using the API to pull vulnerability data into your own tooling, you can now complement that workflow with an out-of-the-box visual experience that makes it easier than ever to understand, prioritize, and act on security findings across your entire BTP landscape.

From API to visual insights - why a UI matters

Security is a team sport. While the API remains the backbone for automated integrations - feeding data into incident management, CI/CD pipelines, or custom dashboards - not everyone on the team speaks JSON. Developers, DevOps engineers, and security managers need a quick, intuitive way to answer questions like:

  • How many vulnerabilities exist across my subaccounts?
  • Which applications carry the highest risk right now?
  • What exactly is affected, and how do I fix it?

The new UI addresses exactly these questions. It is designed to make DevOps teams security-aware without requiring them to build custom reporting first.

What the new UI offers

Findings dashboard with severity breakdown

The landing page gives you a consolidated overview of all vulnerability findings across your subaccounts. A color-coded bar chart breaks down findings by severity (Critical, High, Moderate, Low) per subaccount, so you can immediately spot where attention is needed most.

Below the chart, a searchable and sortable findings table lists every detected vulnerability with its application name, a short description, the maximum CVSS score, severity rating, and the affected subaccount. You can filter by subaccount and space to focus on the areas you are responsible for.

 

Findings dashboard - severity breakdown by subaccount and filterable findings table

Detailed finding view with actionable remediation

Clicking on any finding opens a detail page with three dedicated tabs:

  • General Details: CVSS score, severity, affected packages and versions, CVE identifiers, CVSS vector, source vulnerability ID (e.g. GitHub Security Advisory), and a human-readable description of the vulnerability including, where available, a proof-of-concept.
  • Recommendations: clear, version-specific remediation guidance telling you exactly which version to upgrade to in order to fix the vulnerability.
  • Technical Details: the precise location of the finding: subaccount, Cloud Foundry organization, space, and application name, so you can pinpoint ownership and take action.

This level of detail means you no longer need to cross-reference external vulnerability databases - everything you need to understand and fix the issue is right there in the cockpit.

 

Finding detail view - general details with CVSS score, CVEs, description, and proof of concept

 

Finding detail view - version-specific remediation recommendations and technical details

What powers the findings

Under the hood, nothing has changed about the scanning engine itself - the same proprietary scanning layer that combines commercial scanners, open-source scanners, and SAP-developed MicroScanners continues to analyze your Cloud Foundry applications. This unique combination provides exceptionally broad coverage, including vulnerabilities that may not yet be known to commercial scanning providers alone.

The UI simply gives you a new, visual lens on top of the same rich data that was previously only accessible via the API. And of course, the API remains fully available for all your integration and automation needs.

Get started in 3 steps

Getting up and running with the new UI is straightforward. New subscribers get the UI out of the box. Existing subscribers will have it automatically added within the next few days. Either way, just make sure the correct role collection is assigned to your users.

  1. Subscribe to the service: In the BTP Cockpit, navigate to your subaccount and go to Services → Instances and Subscriptions. Subscribe to the Application Vulnerability Report with the "default" plan.
  2. Assign the role collection: Assign the role collection "Application Vulnerability Report Viewer" to the users who should access the UI. You can do this under Security → Role Collections in the cockpit.
  3. Open the UI: Once subscribed and authorized, open the Application Vulnerability Report from your subscriptions. The "Manage Findings" tile takes you directly to the findings dashboard.

 

Application Vulnerability Report subscription in the BTP Cockpit

For the complete setup documentation, visit the SAP Help Portal. If you are looking for a hands-on walkthrough of the API capabilities, check out the blog post Introducing Application Vulnerability Report for Cloud Foundry Applications – Try It Now!.

Availability

The Application Vulnerability Report (beta) with the new UI is available today on live landscapes. As of now, the service is available in the cf-eu10 region for enterprise accounts.

If you are a new subscriber, the UI is available to you right away. If you are an existing subscriber, the UI will be automatically added to your subscription within the next few days - no action required on your side.

We are actively working on expanding availability to additional regions. In particular, we are looking forward to bringing the service to US landscapes in the near future - stay tuned for updates!

What's next

The UI you see today is our first iteration, and we have ambitious plans to evolve it. Here is a glimpse of what is on our roadmap:

  • More landscapes: expansion to additional regions, with US landscapes as a priority.
  • CALM integration: integration with SAP Cloud ALM for secure lifecycle management, planned for later this year.
  • Continuous improvements: we will keep refining the UI based on your feedback, adding more filtering, grouping, and visualization capabilities.

Try it out and share your feedback!

We built this UI to make vulnerability management on SAP BTP more accessible and actionable. Now we want to hear from you:

  • Does the dashboard give you the overview you need?
  • Are the remediation recommendations helpful?
  • What would you like to see next?

Try the new UI today, and let us know what you think. Your feedback directly shapes how we evolve this service.

If you missed the initial announcement, you can catch up here: Beta Version of Application Vulnerability Report for SAP BTP Now Available.

Also make sure to join our community to learn more about the security services and features in SAP Business Technology Platform: https://community.sap.com/topics/btp-security

You can also check out the road map in SAP Road Map Explorer to see what is coming next.

entry.title: Re: How to set Order price qty tolerance B1/B2 in public...
entry.content:
Good day @Iris59 

Thank you for your question.

Based on your described scenario, the key point you should note is that message M7028 is not controlled by the invoice tolerance settings B1/B2. Those tolerance settings belong to price variance configuration in purchasing, and in Public Cloud they are exposed as SSCUI 101726. They do not provide a standard switch that enable you override the order price quantity shortfall check that raises that message during goods receipt processing. That explains why your two attempts did not work.

The message M7028 appears when the Qty you manually entered in PO Price Unit falls too far below the expected order price quantity. SAP sets the maximum permissible variance at 50%. So, if you manually enter a PO price unit quantity that breaks that expected ratio from the Purchase order conversion, the system will not allow you post.

The Public Cloud supported resolution is not to reconfigure the tolerance to bypass the check (“so I cannot manually add M7028 and set it to W”). In Public Cloud you should make sure that the goods receipt quantity, the order price unit, and the Qty in PO Price Unit stay consistent with the conversion defined in the purchase order or the organisation’s source master data. The Purchasing data model in SAP distinguishes:

  • Quantity in purchase order unit
  • Quantity in purchase order price unit

Which is why manual entry can trigger consistency checks like the one you have experienced.

Consult the following references for more details.

3629511 - Allow Price Change indicator is greyed out in Purchase orders of lean services - SAP for Me

2611207 - SMQ2 Error 'M7028 - Shortfall below order price quantity amounting to more than &1 %' - SAP for Me

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/bb9f1469daf04bd894ab2167f8132a1a/ef7104e005de46de830764293c15d1eb.html?locale=en-US

Below is a quick guide:

  • In Manage Purchase Orders, confirm the ratio between PO quantity and order price unit quantity is correct for the material and supplier setup.

  • Then in Post Goods Receipt for Purchasing Document, open the GR for your PO, let the system automatically derive the Qty in PO Price unit first. Then compare your manually intended value against the value derived by the system. If your manual value creates a shortfall of more than 50%, the system will raise M7028.

If your business really needs a different relationship between received quantity and price unit quantity, then what you need to correct is usually:

  • PO item unit conversion
  • Info record or source price unit setup
  • Material UoM relationship

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: SAP BW Bridge or Data Product Generator (DPG): What would you choose
entry.content: SAP-I extractors work with SAP Datasphere. Extractors without keys can also be done in Replication Flows by enabling semantic keys in the Replication Flow. Delta handling is manual since Datasphere does not have native capabilities like BW to handle different delta images.

entry.title: Re: sap apim timeout issue
entry.content:
Basically it's not recommended to have timeout value of more than 55sec for a proxy. 55 secs has been set as the default IO timeout so that if there is no data to read or if the socket is not ready to write data within 55 secs, the transaction is treated as a timeout. Hence, it is highly recommended to have the default timeout for the proxies [with a timeout of 55 secs] for the complete support from SAP by considering the below options:

1. Optimize the response time of the Backend server so that I/O is happening within 55 seconds.

2. To make asynchronous calls to mitigate the slow responding backend server. Below is the procedure to make an asynchronous call.

• Make a request call and don't wait for a response. This should trigger the processing at your backend.

• Make another API call to check the status of the response from the backend.

• Once the response is ready, then make an API call to fetch the response. This shouldn't take more time as the response is already ready from the backend.

entry.title: Deploying a Production-Ready AI Agent on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry with SAP AI Core Integration
entry.content:
You have built an AI Agent, but how do you deploy it as a secure, scalable enterprise ready instead of a demo running on localhost:8080 ?

As an application developer, you don’t have to build yet another “Hello World” AI application. Instead, you can deploy a real‑world AI Agent on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry that uses SAP AI Core’s Orchestration Service for safety, and enterprise integration.

In this guide, you will:

  • Understand the value of running AI Agent on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry Runtime
  • Explore the AI agent reference application with AI tools calling
  • Deploy the AI agent on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry and see how it behaves in production
  • Configure enterprise authentication with XSUAA and App Router

By the end, you will have a working AI agent application that authenticates users through SAP BTP, calls external tools (embedded in agent), and deploys with a single command on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry Runtime.

Prerequisites

  • SAP BTP account
    • For SAP Developers or Employees: How to create BTP Account For Internal SAP Employee
    • For External Developers, Customers, or Partners: External Developer Setup Tutorial
  • Cloud Foundry 
    • CF CLI v8 to locally interact with the Cloud Foundry environment
    • Cloud MTA Build Tool (MBT) to build andCF MultiApp CLI plugin  to deploy Multitarget Applications (MTAs)
    • CF org and space should be available in the SAP BTP Cloud Foundry environment
  • SAP AI Core
    • Setup SAP AI Core Instance and Service Key, AI Core Tools setup guide
    • An Extended SAP AI Core service plan is required. For more details, refer to SAP AI Core Service Plans

 

Why run AI Agents on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry?

SAP BTP Cloud Foundry runtime is a proven, operational‑grade Platform‑as‑a‑Service (PaaS) for running both traditional and cloud‑native applications. For business applications e.g web frontends, APIs, background services, or integration services, Cloud Foundry already provides:

Runtime abstraction and simplified deployment - With cf push or cf deploy, Cloud Foundry auto‑detects the language runtime (e.g., Python, Node.js, Java), installs dependencies, and manages routes and scaling. Application developers don’t need to manage VMs, Dockerfiles, or other deployment manifests, which keeps the development process focused on business logic instead of infrastructure.

Built‑in service binding and lifecycle management - SAP BTP Cloud Foundry integrates with SAP BTP services like SAP HANA, XSUAA, and SAP Integration Suite. Applications consume them via service instances and bindings, so endpoints and credentials are injected at runtime (e.g., via VCAP_SERVICES). This keeps the application code clean, simplifies configuration, and enables safe blue/green deployments.

Enterprise‑grade security and routing - SAP Cloud Foundry delivers standardized OAuth2‑based authentication and role‑based access control via the App Router and XSUAA services. Application are instantly enterprise‑ready, without custom login flows or token‑validation code


How Cloud Foundry benefits apply to AI Agents ?


AI agents benefit from the same abstraction and simplified deployment - Deploying an AI agent (e.g., a Python application) feels no different from deploying a regular app: Cloud Foundry detects the runtime, installs dependencies, and manages routes and scaling. Your AI agent behaves like “just another app” from an operations perspective, even though it calls SAP AI Core and external tools underneath.

Service binding simplifies AI‑centric architectures - AI‑based agents often depend on multiple services. For instance,

  • SAP AI Core (for the Orchestration Service and LLMs)
  • XSUAA (for authentication)
  • SAP HANA or other data sources (for grounding and retrieval)

       Bind them all, and the AI agent receives connections and credentials automatically (no hard‑coding)

 Security and governance by default - For AI agents, access control and audit-ability are critical. With App Router and XSUAA, you ensure:

  • Only authenticated users can reach the agent
  • Different roles and scopes control who can use which features
  • Requests and application logs are tracked as part of the platform’s standard logging stack

Cloud Foundry’s abstraction, built‑in service binding, and enterprise‑grade security make it ideal not only for traditional business applications but also for AI agents. By treating the AI agents as “just another app” on the platform, developers get the same operational simplicity, security, and integration benefits which are exactly required by AI agents.

 

What are we building: The Reference AI Agent

We are deploying an AI Agent application that has a web interface, showcases production-ready patterns (discussed above) on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry deployments. It connects SAP AI Core, XSUAA and uses external tool calling to:

  • Search the web with DuckDuckGo
  • Query Wikipedia article summaries.
  • Look up word definitions and examples
  • Get real‑time weather from Open‑Meteo

Technology Stack

  • Python Backend: AI Agent with a chat based user interface
  • SAP AI Core integration: leverages unified API for multiple LLMs providers (e.g., GPT‑5)
  • App Router and XSUAA Service: Handles OAuth2 authentication (only authentication)
  • The tools/modules encapsulate external capabilities (tools) that the LLM can call




For local development, see the step-by-step local setup guide in the repository

Step 1: Build the Reference App

# clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/sap-samples/cloud-foundry-runtime-learning-journey.git

# navigate to ai-agent-demo directory
cd cloud-foundry-runtime-learning-journey/examples/ai-samples/ai-agent-demo


Step 2:  Update Configurations


Before we begin with the required configuriation, we need to ensure that the Cloud Foundry org and space is set correctly

# Check your current target
cf target

# If not set, log in and target your org/space
cf login -a https://api.cf.eu12.hana.ondemand.com
cf target -o <your-org> -s <your-space>

# Expected output

API endpoint:   https://api.cf.eu12.hana.ondemand.com
API version:    3
User:           your-email@abccompany.com
Org:            your-org
Space:          your-space


Now, open deploy/xs-security.jsonand deploy/mta.yaml to update configurations:

2.1.  Update  XUSAA redirect routes

Specify the region-specific URL e.g eu12 in deploy/xs-security.json

 

{
  "xsappname": "ai-agent-demo",
  "tenant-mode": "dedicated",
  "oauth2-configuration": {
    "redirect-uris": [
      "https://ai-agent-demo.cfapps.<YOUR_REGION>.hana.ondemand.com/**"
    ]
  }
}


2.2. Update Public Routes 

Find the approuter module indeploy/mta.yaml and update the route with the region determined in the earlier step

 

modules:
  - name: approuter
    type: nodejs
    path: approuter
    parameters:
      memory: 256M
      routes:
        - route: ai-agent-demo.cfapps.<YOUR_REGION>.hana.ondemand.com  # Replace Route with correct region

2.3 Update AI Core Service Configuration

Find the aicore resource indeploy/mta.yamland update the service name

resources:
  - name: aicore
    type: org.cloudfoundry.existing-service
    parameters:
      service-name: default_aicore  # Replace with your AI Core service instance name

 

Step 3: Build the MTA

Now we're ready to build. But Install dependencies first. 

# Install uv if you haven't already
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

# Install dependencies
uv sync

# Activate virtual environment
source .venv/bin/activate  # On Unix/macOS
# or
.venv\Scripts\activate  # On Windows

# sync requirements.txt with pyproject.toml:
uv pip compile pyproject.toml -o requirements.txt

 

Build the Multitarget application (MTA)

cd deploy

mbt build

# Expected output
INFO] the MTA archive generated at: mta_archives/ai-agent-demo_1.0.0.mtar

 

Step 4: Deploying the AI agent to Cloud Foundry (MTA flow)

Deploy the MTAR archive with MTA plugin

cf deploy mta_archives/ai-agent-demo_1.0.0.mtar

What MTA does automatically

  1.  Creates XSUAA service instance (ai-agent-demo-xsuaa)
  2.  Deploys App Router application
  3.  Deploys Python backend application
  4.  Binds XSUAA to both applications
  5.  Binds existing AI Core service instance to backend application
  6.  Configures App Router with backend destination

 

Step 5: Access the AI Agent

Check the status of both the applications and they should be running

 

cf apps

# output

ai-agent-demo                      started           web:1/1     ai-agent-demo_PROTECTED_ROUTE.cfapps.eu12.hana.ondemand.com
approuter                          started           web:1/1     ai-agent-demo.cfapps.eu12.hana.ondemand.com


Now,

  1. Open the URL in your browser - https://<REPLACE_WITH_APPROUTER_URL>.cfapps.eu12.hana.ondemand.com/
  2. You should be redirected to SAP BTP login
  3. After login, you'll see the chat interface

 

Notice that

  • Public Route (App Router): Entry point for users, handles OAuth2 authentication
  • Protected Route (Backend): Direct access is forbidden - only accessible via authenticated requests from App Router
  • Automatic Service Bindings: Authentication credentials from SAP AI Core and XSUAA services are automatically injected

Try these queries and see the results

# Weather
What's the weather in Tokyo?

# Web Search
Search for latest news about SAP AI Core

# Wikipedia
Tell me about machine learning from Wikipedia

# Dictionary
What does serendipity mean?

 

Step 6: Monitoring and Logging Your AI Agent

once deployed, you need visibility into your AI agent's behavior. Cloud Foundry provides built-in logging

6.1 Stream live logs from your applications

# Stream logs from the Python backend
cf logs ai-agent-demo

# Stream logs from App Router
cf logs approuter

# View recent logs (last 100 lines)
cf logs ai-agent-demo --recent


6.2 Key Log Events

 2026-04-20T17:41:04.79+0200 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT === Received input: What's the weather in Tokyo?
   2026-04-20T17:41:09.32+0200 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT === Generated response length: 131
   2026-04-20T17:41:09.32+0200 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT === Response preview: Tools Used: `get_weather`
   2026-04-20T17:41:09.32+0200 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT ---
   2026-04-20T17:41:09.32+0200 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT Weather: Tokyo, Japan
   2026-04-20T17:41:09.32+0200 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT Temperature: 15.6°C
   2026-04-20T17:41:09.32+0200 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT Condition: Partly Cloudy
   2026-04-20T17:41:09.32+0200 [APP/PROC/WEB/0] OUT Wind Speed: 4.6 km/h...


Conclusion

We have learned

  • How AI agent can be securely deployed on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry
  • Developer can  focus on tools, UX, and business logic, while Cloud Foundry and SAP AI Core handle the infrastructure and AI orchestration
  • How to monitor your production AI agent with out of box platform logging provided by Cloud Foundry 

Now, it's time to clone  the reference AI agent Github Repository  tweak it and deploy your own agent on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry.

Helpful Links

    • SAP BTP Cloud Foundry Environment
    • Multitenant Application in SAP BTP Cloud Foundry
    • SAP AI Core Documentation
    • XSUAA (Authorization and Trust Management Service)

entry.title: What Happens to Your Data When You Enable SAP's AI Features?
entry.content:
What Happens to Your Data When You Enable SAP's AI Features?

Ten years ago, I sat in a meeting with a customer who refused to have his SAP Analytics Cloud data stored on AWS. Live connections only. His reasoning was the Patriot Act. US-based infrastructure could potentially be accessed by US authorities, and he wasn't willing to take that risk. Encryption didn't matter. SLAs didn't matter. The data wasn't leaving his control.

I remember thinking he was being careful, maybe a bit extreme, but thorough.

Fast forward to today. Same type of customer, same IT leadership profile. And they're enabling AI features across their SAP landscape that actively send business data to US-based LLMs for processing. Revenue figures, customer names, pricing details. Prompt by prompt.

I'm not saying these are the same thing. Cloud adoption happened, there are contractual safeguards now, and the world moved on. But I do find the contrast interesting. Somewhere along the way we started asking fewer questions. So I thought I'd ask a few.

What I expected

I expected a clean answer. Something like: your data stays in the tenant, Joule processes things locally, nothing leaves the region. That's roughly what most customers assume when I bring it up.

But that's not how it works. At least not exactly.

Joule, the Generative AI Hub, the orchestration service. They rely on third-party LLMs like GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini and various others. All are accessed through SAP AI Core. Your prompt and the business context around it travel to one of these models for inference. 

None of this is hidden. It's in the docs. It just doesn't come up much in enablement sessions.

Simplified, the flow looks like this:

That "IF configured" in the middle is what I want to talk about.

Data masking is a module, not a default

SAP offers data masking through the orchestration service, powered by SAP Data Privacy Integration (DPI).

You have two options:

  • Anonymization: irreversible, the original value is gone.
  • Pseudonymization: reversible, the LLM sees masked tokens and the response is unmasked before you see it.

There's also an allowlist if you want to keep certain terms ("SAP SE", "Harvard University") unmasked. Useful, but every entry is a deliberate hole in your privacy layer.

I confirmed all of this directly with SAP's AI Core Product Management after a conversation at the DSAG Technology Days. They were responsive and pointed me to the documentation. Credit where it's due.

Here's what I want to highlight though: masking is optional. The SDK docs say so plainly. It's a module you configure in the orchestration workflow. If it's not configured, it doesn't do anything.

const orchestrationClient = new OrchestrationClient({
  llm: { model_name: 'gpt-4o' },
  // masking goes HERE, if you add it
  masking: {
    masking_providers: [maskingProvider]
  }
});

In short: No masking block, no masking. SAP's own tutorials describe data masking, content filtering, and translation as "optional orchestration modules." Not defaults.

And this isn't just my read of the docs. Jana Subramanian (SAP's APJ Principal Cybersecurity Advisor) published a detailed security architecture overview of the two inference paths in SAP AI Core. When he describes the orchestration pipeline for LLM calls, he says the guardrails apply "where enabled." That's the same point, from SAP's own side.

The question worth asking in your landscape:

did someone enable them?

What masking catches, and what it doesn't

Say masking is configured. What does it actually catch?

The DPI service recognizes specific entities: person names, email addresses, phone numbers. Classic PII. You can define custom entities with regex, so employee IDs matching EMP-12345 can be masked too.

But think about what actually flows through an SAP system:

  • Next quarter's revenue forecast
  • A customer's contract value
  • Pricing from an ongoing negotiation
  • A strategic project codenamed "Titan"

None of that is PII. None of it triggers standard masking. But it's all commercially sensitive, and most CFOs wouldn't want it sitting on someone else's infrastructure, even transiently.

Unless you build custom regex patterns for every case, this data passes through. In practice, most implementations won't.

[Update pending: I've asked SAP AI Core Product Management directly whether commercially sensitive data beyond PII is covered. I'll update once I hear back.]

The "no training" promise

SAP confirmed that third-party LLM providers don't use your data for model training. This is handled through SAP's vendor onboarding process with standardized contracts.

The phrasing was that "opting out on the 'use data for training' is possible" through these agreements. Note the word "possible." It's careful wording.

This is a contractual guarantee, not a technical one. Your data still reaches the LLM provider's infrastructure and gets processed there. It's just not retained or used for training afterwards.

For most customers, that's fine. But my careful customer from ten years ago would probably ask: what happens if the contract changes? If the vendor gets acquired? If a subprocessor enters the chain?

Those aren't paranoid questions. They're standard DPO questions.

[Update pending: I've also asked whether this opt-out is the default for all SAP-served models, or whether each customer needs to request it.]

There are other options (and they might be enough)

Here's something I think gets lost in the Joule excitement: not every use case needs GPT-4o.

SAP AI Core distinguishes between models hosted on AWS Bedrock, Azure OpenAI, or GCP Vertex AI (all US hyperscalers), and models that are "hosted and managed by SAP" directly. Mistral falls in the second group. So do IBM, Nvidia, and a set of open-source models.

For a lot of common tasks (summarization, classification, simple Q&A, document generation with templates) an open-source or EU-hosted model is often enough. Sometimes more than enough. And if you want to go further, you can run your own open-source model entirely on your own infrastructure or in a self-managed BTP deployment. No third-party LLM provider in the chain at all.

This is the part I'd push on if you're in a regulated industry or a Mittelstand company with strict data governance: the "use a US hyperscaler LLM" path is the default, but it's not the only path. The orchestration layer's harmonized API makes switching models a configuration change, not a rewrite.

Use what you have. Validate what you need.

[Update pending: And I've asked about inference location for EU-hosted customers. I'll add their answer here.]

What I'd actually do

If you're evaluating or already running AI features in your SAP landscape, here's my practical checklist:

  1. Ask your BTP team whether data masking is configured in the orchestration layer, or whether you're running on defaults. If they're not sure, you have your answer.
  2. Map your sensitive data beyond PII. Revenue numbers, customer names in business context, project codes, pricing. Then check if any of it is actually covered by your masking configuration.
  3. Understand the full data path. From SAP application, through the orchestration layer, to the LLM, and back. Know which model, where it's hosted, and what's contractual vs. architectural.
  4. Look at SAP-hosted or open-source models before defaulting to GPT-4o. A smaller EU-hosted model may fit your use case and your compliance requirements better.
  5. Check what your policy actually says. "No training" and "no transmission" aren't the same thing. Make sure you're meeting the real requirement.
  6. Make it an explicit decision. Whether you enable masking or skip it, document the choice. The worst outcome is a default that no one thought about.

Why now

Joule is being embedded across the SAP product suite. The Generative AI Hub is becoming a core platform capability. AI in SAP isn't experimental anymore.

That's why this conversation is worth having now instead of after everyone has enabled these features. SAP has built the building blocks for responsible AI usage. But building blocks need assembly, and right now the instructions are scattered across SDK docs, help portal pages, and conference hallway conversations.

My customer from ten years ago wouldn't host his SAC data on AWS. Today, that same level of care would probably serve a lot of organizations well. Not to block progress. Just to know what you're agreeing to when you click "enable."

Have you turned on Joule or the Generative AI Hub? What questions came up, or didn't? I'd genuinely like to hear how other teams are approaching this. 

Links:

  • SAP AI Core, Data Masking
  • SAP Data Privacy Integration
  • SAP AI Core, Security
  • Secure Data Flow for AI Inference on SAP BTP: RPT-1 vs LLM Orchestration
  • SAP Cloud SDK for AI, Orchestration
  • SAP Tutorial, Data Masking in Orchestration
