entry.title: How can I add BOM items to a header using F6578 without supplying an engineering change document?
entry.content:
Hello,

since CEWB gets discontinued in the future we are looking for alternatives. I found the SAP note 3658447 that gives 3 apps that should do the same:

  • F6863 Mass maintenance of bill of material headers
  • F6578 Mass maintenance of bill of material items
  • F6862 Mass addition of bill of material items

We managed to get around a quirk with numerical material numbers with F6578 where you have to add leading zeros to the "to add" material number when switching components.

However we hit a new road block with F6862 when adding material items to a BOM. Here I get a cryptic error message "Property 'ENGINEERINGCHANGEDOCUMENT' is invalid". 

Steps:

  1. enter material number into the filter area
  2. select entry
  3. click "add item to Selected BOM Headers" or "add item to All BOM Headers"
  4. item category = Stock Items (L)
  5. supply component number (with and without leading zeros), component quantity and component UoM
  6. click Simulate 

When checking /IWFND/ERROR_LOG the error comes from the fact that the property is emtpy and can't be decoded. The log contains the exceptions 

  1. /IWCOR/CX_OD_BAD_REQUEST with the message "The Data Services Request could not be understood due to malformed syntax"
  2. CX_SXML_PARSE_ERROR with "Error while parsing an XML stream" 
  3. /IWCOR/CX_OD_EP_PROPERTY_ERROR with "Property 'ENGINEERINGCHANGEDOCUMENT' is invalid"

The request data body contains "ENGINEERINGCHANGEDOCUMENT" : "".

SAP ECM isn't used/activated in our system and I can add the material item to the BOM using transaction CS02 with no problem and without supplying any engineering change document number. I even created a change document number using CC01 and ticked all boxes in the object types subscreen to ensure this change number is valid for all operations but even then with this number supplied I still get the same error.

Fiori App Library for this app shows this SAP Help article which did not give any further help. 

So can I even add material items to a bom using this Fiori app without a engineering change document? And if I can add material items to the bom how can I avoid this error?

Additional information:

  • SAP S/4Hana
  • 2022 FPS02

entry.title: RAP Unmanaged Implementation: Draft Enablement, Late Numbering, and Associations Explained.
entry.content:
Introduction.

SAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) provides a powerful framework for building modern, scalable applications. While managed scenarios offer built-in handling of persistence and standard operations, unmanaged scenarios give developers greater flexibility to implement custom business logic and control the complete data flow manually.

In this blog, we will explore how to build an unmanaged RAP Business Object, focusing on key concepts such as Late Numbering, Draft Handling, and Header-Item associations.

LATE NUMBERING:

           In Late Numbering the primary key (usually ID) of a business object is not generated at the time of creation, but instead assigned later during the save (persist) phase, this is typically handled inside the SAVE method of the behavior implementation class.
So initially:

  • Temporary keys are used during transaction processing.
  • Final keys are generated only when data is committed to the database.

Late numbering is useful in scenarios where:

  • Number depends on final validation or logic
    Example: Generate ID only after all validations pass
  • Avoid number gaps
    If numbering happens early and transaction fails → gaps occur. Late numbering ensures numbers are assigned only for successful transactions

How It Works (Conceptually)

  • User creates record → temporary key assigned
  • Data stays in transactional buffer

During SAVE:

  • Generate final number
  • Replace temporary key with actual key
  • Save to database

DRAFT:

Draft handling allows users to save incomplete data without activating it in the database.
It enables:

  • “Save as Draft”
  • “Edit Later”
  • “Activate when ready”

This is widely used in Fiori applications.

Why Do We Use Draft?

  • User convenience
       Users can partially fill data and come back later.
  • Avoid data loss
       Work is not lost if session ends.

How Draft Works (Flow):

  • User creates or edits data → stored in Draft table.
  • Draft data is isolated from active data.
  • User can:
    1. Edit draft
    2. Discard draft
    3. Activate draft

On Activate:

  • Draft data moves to active table.
  • Business logic (validations, determinations) is triggered.

Number Assignment in Draft with Late Numbering:
What happens to the ID in Draft?

When we use Late Numbering + Draft, the actual (final) key is NOT assigned in the draft table.

Instead:

  • A temporary key (UUID or %CID) is used internally.
  • The real number is assigned only during Activate (SAVE).

There will be two phase:
      Draft Phase and Activation Phase.
During Draft (Create / Edit).

  • No final number is generated
    RAP uses:
    1. Temporary key (%CID) in backend processing
    2. Draft UUID (technical key) in draft tables
    3. The business key field is usually:
          Empty OR Filled with a placeholder (optional, but not final)

So draft data is stored without the actual business number

During Activation (Save)

  • SAVE method is triggered
  • You generate the final number
    System:
    1. Replaces temporary key with actual key
    2. Moves data from draft → active table

SCENARIO:

In this application, we have a Product (Header) table (ZGN_T_PRODUCTS) and an Order (Item) table (ZGN_T_ORDERS) linked through productid. Users can create and save Product records independently, while Orders can be created later based on the product. The requirement is to generate both Product ID and Order ID only during final save (activation) using late numbering. Users should also be able to save incomplete data as draft and complete it later. To handle custom logic, and manual persistence, an unmanaged RAP approach is used

1.Database tables:

@EndUserText.label : 'Product table - Header'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zgn_t_products {

  key client    : abap.clnt not null;
  key productid : abap.char(3) not null;
  productname   : abap.char(15);
  quantity      : abap.char(3);
  price         : abap.char(7);
  totalprice    : abap.char(10);
  lastchange    : timestampl;

}

 

@EndUserText.label : 'Order table - Item'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zgn_t_orders {

  key client    : abap.clnt not null;
  key orderid   : abap.char(3) not null;
  key productid : abap.char(3) not null;
  orderstatus   : abap.char(10);
  location      : abap.char(20);

}

2. Created draft tables for both Product (Header) and Order (Item) entities to support draft functionality. These tables store intermediate data, allowing users to save incomplete records and activate them later.

@EndUserText.label : 'Draft table for entity ZGN_I_PRODUCTS'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #EXTENSIBLE_ANY
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zgn_dft_prdts {

  key mandt     : mandt not null;
  key productid : abap.char(3) not null;
  key draftuuid : sdraft_uuid not null;
  productname   : abap.char(15);
  quantity      : abap.char(3);
  price         : abap.char(7);
  totalprice    : abap.char(10);
  lastchange    : timestampl;
  "%admin"      : include sych_bdl_draft_admin_inc;

}
@EndUserText.label : 'Draft table for entity ZGN_I_ORDERS'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #EXTENSIBLE_ANY
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zgn_dft_order {

  key mandt       : mandt not null;
  key orderid     : abap.char(3) not null;
  key productid   : abap.char(3) not null;
  key draftuuid   : sdraft_uuid not null;
  parentdraftuuid : sdraft_uuid;
  orderstatus     : abap.char(10);
  location        : abap.char(20);
  "%admin"        : include sych_bdl_draft_admin_inc;

}

3. Created an Interface view on top of these two database table. Defined a proper association between Product (Header) and Order (Item) using productid to establish the relationship.

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Product interface view'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define root view entity ZGN_I_PRODUCTS as select from ZGN_T_PRODUCTS
composition[1..*] of ZGN_I_ORDERS as _order
{
    key productid as Productid,
    productname as Productname,
    quantity as Quantity,
    price as Price,
    totalprice as Totalprice,
    lastchange as Lastchange,
    _order
}
@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Order interface view'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define view entity ZGN_I_ORDERS as select from zgn_t_orders
association to parent ZGN_I_PRODUCTS as _products
on $projection.Productid = _products.Productid
{
    key orderid as Orderid,
    key productid as Productid,
    orderstatus as Orderstatus,
    location as Location,
    _products
}

4.  Created a consumption CDS view on top of the interface views to expose the data for UI usage. Instead of using metadata extensions, all required UI annotations were directly maintained within the consumption view for simplicity.

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Product consumption view'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define root view entity ZGN_C_PRODUCTS
  provider contract transactional_query
   as projection on ZGN_I_PRODUCTS
{
       .facet: [{ position: 1,
    type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE,
                  purpose: #STANDARD,
                  label: 'Product details' },
                  
                  {
                  position: 2,
                  type: #LINEITEM_REFERENCE,
                  purpose: #STANDARD,
                  label: 'Order details',
                  targetElement: '_order'
                  } ]
                  
    
    .lineItem: [{ position: 10 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 10, label : 'Product id' }]
    key Productid,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 20 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 20, label : 'Product Name' }]
    Productname,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 30 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 30, label : 'Quantity' }]
    Quantity,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 40 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 40, label : 'Price' }]
    Price,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 50 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 50, label : 'Total price' }]
    Totalprice,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 60 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 10, label : 'Last change' }]
    Lastchange,
    _order : redirected to composition child ZGN_C_ORDERS

}
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Order projection view'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define view entity ZGN_C_ORDERS as projection on ZGN_I_ORDERS
{
     .facet: [{ position: 1,
    type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE,
                  purpose: #STANDARD,
                  label: 'Order details' }]
                  
    .lineItem: [{ position: 10 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 10, label : 'Order id' }]
    key Orderid,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 20 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 20, label : 'Product id' }]
    key Productid,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 30 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 30, label : 'Order status' }]
    Orderstatus,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 40 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 40, label : 'Location' }]
    Location,
    /* Associations */
    _products : redirected to parent zgn_c_products
}

5. Created behavior definitions on top of the interface views for both Product (Header) and Order (Item) entities. Defined the required operations and enabled features like draft handling, associations, and late numbering as per our requirements.

unmanaged implementation in class zbp_gn_i_products unique;
strict ( 2 );
with draft;

define behavior for ZGN_I_PRODUCTS //alias <alias_name>
draft table zgn_dft_prdts
late numbering
lock master
total etag Lastchange
authorization master ( instance )

{
  create;
  update;
  delete;
  field ( readonly ) Productid, Totalprice;
  association _order { create; with draft; }
  draft action Activate optimized;
  draft action Edit;
  draft action Resume;
  draft action Discard;
  draft determine action Prepare;

  determination total on modify { field Price; field Quantity; }
  side effects
  { field Price affects field Totalprice;
    field Quantity affects field Totalprice; }

  mapping for zgn_t_products
    {
      Productid   = productid;
      Productname = productname;
      Quantity    = quantity;
      Price       = price;
      Totalprice  = totalprice;
      Lastchange  = lastchange;
    }

}

define behavior for ZGN_I_ORDERS //alias <alias_name>
draft table zgn_dft_order
late numbering
lock dependent by _products
authorization dependent by _products
{
  update;
  delete;
  field ( readonly : update ) Orderid, Productid;
  association _products { with draft; }

  mapping for zgn_t_orders
    {
      Orderid     = orderid;
      Productid   = productid;
      Orderstatus = orderstatus;
      Location    = location;
    }

}

 6. Implemented the required business logic in the behavior pool class using a helper class. This includes handling late numbering, calculations, and manual data persistence for both Product and Order entities.

    In the helper class, created a dedicated method to handle late numbering logic. This method generates the Product ID and Order ID and is called from the behavior pool adjust_numbers method.

 METHODS late_num CHANGING mapped   TYPE tt_late
                              reported TYPE tt_report_late  .

 METHOD late_num.
    IF gt_tab IS NOT INITIAL.
      SELECT FROM zgn_t_products
      FIELDS
      MAX( productid )
      INTO (lv_max).
      LOOP AT gt_tab ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_max>).
        lv_max += 1.
        <fs_max>-productid = lv_max.
      ENDLOOP.
    ENDIF.

    IF gt_tab_item1 IS NOT INITIAL.
      SELECT FROM zgn_t_orders
     FIELDS
     MAX( orderid )
     INTO (lv_max_i).
      LOOP AT gt_tab_item1 ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_max_i>).
        lv_max_i += 1.
        <fs_max_i>-orderid = lv_max_i.
      ENDLOOP.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.

     Called this method in the behavior pool class

  METHOD adjust_numbers.
    zcl_gn_helper_unmln=>get_inst(  )->late_num(
      CHANGING
        mapped   = mapped
        reported = reported
    ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD save.
    zcl_gn_helper_unmln=>get_inst(  )->save_data(
     CHANGING
       reported = reported
   ).
  ENDMETHOD.

Implemented Persistance Logic for Header and Item.

Implemented the create methods for both Product (Header) and Order (Item) in the behavior implementation.

  • In the header create method (create_data), the incoming entity data is mapped to an internal table using CORRESPONDING, which is later used for persistence.
  • In the item create method (create_item):
    1. The Product ID from the header is captured.
    2. Item data is extracted using %target.
    3. A loop is used to assign the Product ID to each item, maintaining the header- item association.
    4. Finally, the data is prepared for database insertion and mapped back to the response structure.

    This ensures that all order items are correctly linked to their respective product during creation.

%target is used in composition (Header → Item) scenarios to access the child entity data (Item records) that come along with the parent during a deep create.

 METHOD create_data.
    gt_tab = CORRESPONDING #( entities MAPPING FROM ENTITY ).
  ENDMETHOD.

METHOD create_item.
    DATA(lv_pid) = entities[ 1 ]-Productid.
    gt_tab_item = CORRESPONDING #( entities[ 1 ]-%target ).

    LOOP AT gt_tab_item ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_item>).
      <fs_item>-productid = lv_pid.
    ENDLOOP.

    APPEND VALUE zgn_t_orders( productid = <fs_item>-productid
                               orderid = <fs_item>-orderid
                               orderstatus = <fs_item>-orderstatus
                               location = <fs_item>-location )
                        TO gt_tab_item1.
    mapped-zgn_i_orders = CORRESPONDING #( entities ).
  ENDMETHOD.

Output:

During product creation, the Product ID is not assigned initially and remains empty in draft mode; it is generated during the save (activation) phase through late numbering.

After clicking on create:

Same applies for Order ID, which remains blank during creation and gets generated upon save

The below image shows the draft table entries for Orders, where the data is temporarily stored during the draft phase. At this stage, the Order ID is not yet finalized, and the system maintains a draft UUID to uniquely identify the record.

After clicking on save the order id gets generated.

The below screen shows the active data after clicking Save (activation). Both Product ID (Header) and Order ID (Item) are now successfully generated using late numbering. The Product and its associated Orders are persisted in the database with proper header-item linkage via productid.

Conclusion:

In this blog, we explored how to implement an unmanaged RAP Business Object with key features like Late Numbering, Draft Handling, and Header-Item association. We saw how both Product (Header) and Order (Item) records can be created in draft mode and how their IDs are generated only during the save (activation) phase using late numbering. By handling the logic in the behavior pool and helper class, we achieved full control over data persistence, and associations.

entry.title: AI in Healthcare: Inviting Ideas, Challenges, and Perspectives
entry.content:
Reimagining Healthcare with AI: A Call for Collaboration

In today’s rapidly evolving healthcare landscape, organizations are undergoing a significant transformation driven by data, automation, and Artificial Intelligence (AI). From improving patient outcomes to optimizing operations, AI is becoming a key enabler of innovation across the industry.

As part of the CIS Innovation Incubation team, we are working on building AI-driven use cases to enhance efficiency, improve decision-making, and deliver value to healthcare providers, partners, and stakeholders.

Why Healthcare?

Healthcare plays a critical role in society, covering areas such as patient care, supply chain management, compliance, quality assurance, and operational planning. However, many processes still involve manual effort, fragmented systems, and delayed insights.

With the increasing volume of data - from clinical records to operational systems - there is a strong opportunity for AI to:

  • Unlock actionable insights from complex datasets
  • Reduce manual and repetitive tasks
  • Improve speed and accuracy of decision-making
  • Enable more proactive and predictive operations

Our Objective

We are focusing on:

  • Identifying real-world pain points in healthcare processes
  • Understanding challenges faced by customers and partners
  • Co-creating impactful AI-driven solutions

Potential Areas of Innovation

Some of the broader areas where AI can create value in healthcare include:

  • Intelligent data analysis for faster insights
  • Predictive analytics for better planning and resource utilization
  • Automation of document-heavy and repetitive workflows
  • Conversational assistants for quick access to information
  • Real-time monitoring for risk and compliance

Collaboration Approach

To ensure meaningful impact, we are adopting a collaborative approach:

  • Engaging with Product Managers and healthcare teams
  • Conducting discussions to gather inputs and pain points
  • Running initial development in parallel for rapid prototyping

Call for Inputs

We would like to hear from the community:

  • What are the key challenges you face in healthcare today?
  • Which processes are time-consuming or error-prone?
  • Where do you see the highest potential for AI adoption?

Your inputs will help us build meaningful and scalable solutions.

Next Steps

Based on the feedback, we will prioritize use cases, develop prototypes, and iterate with stakeholders.

Conclusion

AI has the potential to transform healthcare from reactive operations to proactive, data-driven decision-making. Collaboration will be key to achieving this transformation.

Looking forward to your inputs.

entry.title: Create supplier invoice app and import supplier invoice app does not flow to DRC
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

We noticed that when we use "Create Supplier Invoice" app and "Import Supplier Invoice" app to create the Finance invoices, it doesn't flow to SAP DRC.

However, it is working fine if using "Create Incoming Invoices" app. For MM invoices from "Create Supplier Invoice -Advanced" app, there is no issue as well. It is able to flow to SAP DRC.

Anyone has any thoughts?

Thanks!

entry.title: Beyond Joule Capabilities: A Knowledge Graph Driven Runtime for SAP Business AI
entry.content:
Beyond Joule Capabilities: A Knowledge Graph Driven Runtime for SAP Business AI

A working prototype and the idea behind it

IN THIS POST

  1. The scaling question
  2. The core idea
  3. The example
  4. Why this is different from Joule capabilities
  5. The architecture - KG as runtime brain
  6. Users do not think in system boundaries
  7. BDC - a potential path to faster coverage
  8. The honest complexity
  9. The prototype
  10. Closing thoughts

The scaling question

Today, building AI on SAP often means building capabilities one use case at a time. A new question introduces a new YAML definition, API binding, and deployment cycle. This model works well for many scenarios. But it raises a natural question: how does it scale as the variety and cross-system reach of user questions increases?

SAP's platform invitation is explicit: Joule as "an AI agent orchestrator," an agent builder for custom deployments, Business Data Cloud to "supercharge AI agents with the data that matters most, from any source." The infrastructure is there. This post explores one possible model for what to build on top of it - a working prototype running on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry, using a real A2A endpoint and a live PostgreSQL knowledge graph to answer retrieval questions without per-use-case authoring.

This is a personal exploration, not a product announcement. All views are my own. One constraint upfront: this covers read-oriented scenarios only - retrieval, explanation, analytics. Transactional write operations are out of scope.

The core idea

Joule owns the conversation. A2A owns the routing. The knowledge graph owns proven access patterns. OData owns the live data.

Each layer has one job. When a new system is added or a business term changes, only the relevant layer changes. The rest stays untouched.

This separation of concerns is the architectural backbone  and the reason the model handles question variability differently than a capability-per-use-case approach. The knowledge graph accumulates validated OData patterns over time; the A2A runtime draws on them at request time. Questions the KG already knows how to answer require no new authoring.

The example

A user says to Joule: "Show me my active purchase orders from supplier Bosch." No capability was pre-authored for that question.

Here is what happens:

 

Fig 1. The KG holds the pattern. The A2A layer matches the intent and composes the filter at runtime. Joule sees a card identical to any native capability.

The KG held the base pattern for active purchase orders. The slot extractor identified "Bosch" from the utterance and composed the OData filter at runtime.

The actual call:

The response template stored alongside the pattern in the KG rendered those records as a Joule card. The seam between Joule and the agent is invisible to the end user.

This leads to an important distinction from how Joule capabilities are typically built today.

Why this is different from Joule capabilities

SAP's Architecture Center documents two paths for extending Joule with custom agents: low-code via Joule Studio, and pro-code via the Bring Your Own Agent pattern -  where you expose an A2A-compliant HTTP endpoint and register it as a Joule Scenario. This prototype follows the pro-code path. What the knowledge graph adds is a runtime resolution layer on top of it: instead of one endpoint per use case, one endpoint resolves across all patterns the KG knows.

            TRADITIONAL JOULE CAPABILITY

             A2A + KNOWLEDGE GRAPH MODEL

  • One YAML file per use case, authored upfront
  • Hardcoded API, filter, and field selection
  • New question type = new capability version
  • Scale = more YAML
 
  • Patterns live in the KG, not in YAML files
  • Slot extraction composes filters at runtime
  • New supplier, year, or document: same pattern handles all
  • Scale = more usage
 

When to Use Each Approach

  • Traditional Joule Capabilities: Simple, well-defined use cases; single system queries; predictable user patterns
  • KG-Driven Runtime: Cross-system queries; high variation scenarios; exploratory business questions

The shift is from design-time authoring to runtime resolution. Governance changes accordingly: instead of "who owns this YAML?" the question becomes "who validated this OData pattern?" - answered by a domain lead approving a query-and-result pair, not a developer editing files.

Reference: SAP Architecture Center -  Integrating AI Agents with Joule documents the Bring Your Own Agent pro-code path, including A2A v0.3.0 protocol requirements and Joule Scenario registration.

The architecture - KG as runtime brain

Full architecture - A2A + Knowledge Graph on SAP BTP

Fig 2. End-to-end architecture: Joule routes via A2A to the self-hosted agent on BTP CF; the Knowledge Graph resolves intent and composes OData filters at runtime. Dashed boxes are planned / not yet GA.

 

 


Fig 3. The A2A layer is thin routing. The KG is where intelligence accumulates. The factory and runtime are the same flywheel - capability builds deposit patterns; the A2A runtime draws on them.

SAP's pro-code reference architecture for BTP agents also names a Knowledge Graph Engine - part of HANA Cloud - as the layer for encoding semantic business relationships. The PostgreSQL KG here is a developer-accessible analog of that concept: the same idea of accumulated, semantically enriched access patterns, running on a BTP-bound managed service rather than HANA Cloud.

Users do not think in system boundaries

"What is our total spend with supplier Bosch this year?" That question touches S/4HANA purchase orders, Ariba contracts, and Concur expense claims simultaneously. Users ask it as one question. Today it typically requires three separate lookups.

A2A offers a path toward answering it in one response - routing to specialized agents per system at runtime and composing the result. Each agent knows its own domain; none needs to know about the others. The same pattern applies across the SAP portfolio: procurement in S/4HANA, sourcing in Ariba, workforce in SuccessFactors, expense in Concur, external workforce in Fieldglass. The A2A routing layer is what makes cross-system answers possible without cross-system coupling.

One practical challenge emerges quickly: how to build initial KG coverage.

BDC - a potential path to faster coverage

The model works without BDC. But adoption will be slow. Building coverage through validated OData executions alone is a gradual process, and until the KG has enough patterns, too many intents fall back to "not yet supported."

BDC Data Products are designed to expose semantically enriched, consumption-ready models covering business definitions, domain taxonomy, and cross-entity relationships across SAP applications. If those models can be used to seed provisional KG entries, and live OData executions then promote them to validated, the cold-start problem becomes more manageable. Whether that seeding pipeline is straightforward to build depends on how BDC exposes its metadata in a given tenant, something worth exploring rather than assuming.

Raw OData tells you the shape of the data. BDC aims to tell you what the data means. That distinction could matter significantly when matching natural language to a query.

BDC also points toward a second channel - analytical questions that are difficult or impractical to answer through transactional OData alone, such as aggregated spend analysis or trend queries across periods. Those typically require pre-computed or aggregated data rather than a live API call. This is a natural conceptual extension of the same A2A routing layer, though not yet built in this prototype.

One important caveat: BDC's semantic model could provide a useful starting point, but tenant-specific customizations, restricted entity sets, and version differences would still require live validation before any pattern is trusted at runtime. BDC reduces the cold-start problem, it does not eliminate the validation step.

The honest complexity

Three areas are harder in practice than the architecture suggests.

Intent matching

PostgreSQL full-text search on intent keywords is fast but brittle on synonyms: "open orders" and "not invoiced" can mean the same thing; "cancelled" means different things in different modules. Production-grade matching would benefit from hybrid search combining full-text with embedding-based vector similarity so that semantic proximity compensates for lexical gaps.

Slot extraction

Resolving "Bosch" from a free-text utterance to a SupplierID sounds straightforward. In practice, users type partial names, aliases, or misspellings. Production-grade slot resolution needs alias handling, fuzzy matching, and potentially a lookup against master data, making it functionally a mini-MDM problem.

Authorization

The prototype authenticates via XSUAA JWT but calls OData using a service token, meaning it does not enforce row-level authorization per user. The right production path is principal propagation through BTP Destination Service - running the OData call under the user's own identity. This is on the roadmap but not yet implemented. The SAP Reference Architecture notes that the production Agent Gateway path uses IAS App2App tokens with named user context; the delta between that and a service token is the same gap this prototype still needs to close.

                                What works well

                                       Real limitations

  • Deterministic: every answer traces to a validated OData path - no hallucinated field names
  • Fast: KG lookup + OData call + render is sub-second
  • Auditable: every response traces to a specific pattern, entity, and filter
  • Composable: each system's agent is independently owned and evolvable

 

  • Cold start: BDC seeding may help; without some form of pre-seeding, early coverage is thin
  • Intent matching: full-text search alone misfires on synonyms and ambiguous phrasing
  • Slot resolution: aliases and fuzzy matching needed for production-grade accuracy
  • Authorization: row-level security requires principal propagation - not yet implemented
  • Schema drift: S/4HANA upgrades can silently break existing patterns
 

 

Governance model (in the prototype): Patterns are proposed automatically when a query succeeds. A domain lead reviews and promotes to validated=true before the pattern is trusted at runtime. Validation evidence - query, response, timestamp, user context - is logged against each pattern. Patterns not executed in N days are flagged for review, not deleted.

The prototype

Running on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry: Next.js application, SAP AppRouter for XSUAA authentication, PostgreSQL knowledge graph, BTP Destination Service for OData connectivity.

 

Context: SAP's official path for custom agents connecting to Joule is the Agent Gateway - documented in the SAP Architecture Center as exposing Joule Agents via A2A 0.3.0 with IAS App2App authentication. The Agent Gateway is not yet generally available. This prototype takes the developer path in the interim: a self-hosted A2A endpoint on BTP CF using XSUAA JWT. When the Agent Gateway reaches GA, the authentication model and registration process would change - the KG and OData runtime layer underneath would not.

Fig 4. * Service token today; principal propagation (user identity through to OData) is on the roadmap. When Agent Gateway reaches GA, the entry point shifts from self-hosted AppRouter to SAP-managed endpoint - the KG and OData layers remain unchanged.

Dual protocol handler

The endpoint accepts both A2A JSON-RPC 2.0 and Joule's native agent-request format. Both converge on the same resolver:

 

The resolver: KG lookup, slot extraction, OData, render

 

The knowledge graph schema

Joule's contextId field carries pagination state across conversation turns. When a user says "show more," the skip offset and pattern ID are encoded in the context ID,  so the next turn re-runs the same OData query from the correct offset without a new KG lookup.

Closing thoughts

The pieces fit together reasonably well. The A2A protocol is documented and implemented in Joule. The OData catalog is rich and well-governed. BTP handles authentication and connectivity. The engineering gaps - vector search, slot resolution, principal propagation are real but well-defined.

For teams building on Joule and A2A today, this suggests a possible shift in focus: from defining individual capabilities to curating reusable, validated access patterns. The two approaches are not mutually exclusive ; capability builds are what populate the KG in the first place. But the longer-term value may compound differently when patterns are shared rather than siloed per use case.

The idea to sit with

This is not a replacement for Joule capabilities. It is a complementary layer - one that may make them more scalable over time.

The real question is not can this work?
It is: who owns the knowledge graph?

One possible direction for SAP AI is not simply more capabilities, but fewer, more adaptive ones backed by a shared knowledge layer that agents draw on at runtime. SAP is delivering the ingredients: Joule as orchestrator, A2A as the routing standard, BDC as the semantic foundation.

Who else is exploring this direction?

This post reflects a personal exploration and learning exercise building on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry. All views are my own and do not represent SAP's positions, roadmap, or product direction. Code snippets are from a personal prototype and are shared for illustrative purposes only.

Co-Authored with J4C and Sonnet 🙂

entry.title: SAP MDG + Azure AD - Part 1: The Why, The Gap, and What Becomes Possible
entry.content:
Hi Peers, I'm Prem, an SAP MDG Techno-Functional Consultant. This is my first blog on SCN, and I wanted to share something genuine gap and a to be use case in MDG. I didn't want to rewrite SAP documentation. Instead, I aimed to provide a real insight into something I got my hands on, figured out, ran into issues and ultimately got working.

In one of the client projects I'm working on, we needed to sync user information from the client's Azure Active Directory with SAP. This meant keeping identity data up to date across both systems. The business wanted user profiles, contact details, and organizational data from Azure AD to be accessible in the MDG landscape without having data stewards enter it manually each time. The question was clear: how do we get SAP to read from Azure Active Directory? This led me to the ABAP SDK for Azure - an open-source toolkit supported by Microsoft. What began as a simple exploration turned into a full end-to-end integration. We set up SAP to authenticate with Azure AD using OAuth2, call the Microsoft Graph API, and pull live user data into the system.

The setup had its share of trial and errors, a few configuration missteps, some back and forth with IDs and certificates. What helped me get through it was the ABAP SDK for Azure repository on GitHub and the work its contributors have put into the documentation and demo programs. Solid resource - I'd recommend bookmarking it before you start

However after all these hiccups it works now. Throughout this process, I gained a strong Introductory Understanding of OAuth2, SSL certificate management, cloud identity concepts, and, most importantly, what Azure Active Directory is and how it fits into an SAP MDG landscape.

Now, what is Azure Active Directory?

Azure Active Directory recently renamed Microsoft Entra ID is where most companies manage their employee identities. Each person in the organization has a profile there, including their name, email, department, job title, manager, and the applications they can access. It serves as the single source of truth for who works in the organization and what they are allowed to do. If SAP MDG is where your organization manages critical business master data, Azure AD is where it manages people data. Connecting the two ensures that your master data in SAP stays current and accurate, just like your HR directory.

The Real Gap

Azure AD has the people data, while SAP MDG has the business data. Both are within the same organization, but they do not communicate. Data stewards manually bridge this gap, which is not a data quality issue. It is an integration gap.

 

 

 

What becomes possible once SAP can communicate with Azure AD?

Here’s what opens up in MDG with this connection:

  • User onboarding: When a new employee is created in Azure AD, SAP reads that profile and automatically provisions their MDG roles and workflow assignments. No tickets or waiting involved.
  • Business Partner enrichment: Information like job title, department, phone number, and reporting manager resides in Azure AD. Instead of manual typing by data stewards, the system pulls this information directly. It becomes accurate and requires no manual input.
  • Contact validation: If a data steward enters a contact person on a vendor record, the system validates it against Azure AD in real time. It checks whether this person exists in the organization. If not, it raises a flag before the change request can proceed.
  • Dynamic workflow routing: Rather than having fixed approvers, the workflow reads the requestor's manager directly from Azure AD. If the approver changes roles, the routing automatically adjusts.
  • Access governance: Periodically comparing MDG role assignments with Azure AD helps identify if someone has left the organization but still holds active MDG roles. These roles can then be flagged for removal. No more inactive users remaining in governance roles.

Five use cases, one integration, one endpoint.

 

What we built and how it works

The technical setup involves SAP authenticating with Azure AD, receiving an OAuth2 token, and using that token to call the Microsoft Graph API. It requires one RFC destination for authentication, another for the API, three configuration tables, and the ABAP SDK for Azure manages the rest.

I will guide you through every step including every mistake I have gone through in the upcoming parts of this series.

What’s next?

This was the why part & Part 2 will cover the how. You'll see a complete technical walkthrough, from creating an Azure account to seeing user data appear in your SAP system

 Stay tuned.

entry.title: How to configure SAP JAVA LDAP using port 8443.
entry.content:
Hello Team,

In this blog, I will describe how to configure JAVA LDAP using port 8443.

Overview of Java LDAP Using Port 8443 in SAP.

In SAP environments, LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) is commonly used for user authentication, authorization, and directory services. When SAP Java systems communicate securely with an LDAP directory, SSL/TLS is typically involved. In some SAP landscapes, this secure communication is configured to run over port 8443.

What Is LDAP in SAP Java?

In SAP NetWeaver AS Java, LDAP integration allows SAP to:

Authenticate users against a central directory (Active Directory / LDAP server)
Synchronize users and groups
Enable Single Sign-On (SSO)
Centralize user management instead of maintaining users locally in SAP
SAP Java uses its User Management Engine (UME) to connect to LDAP servers.

 

By default, the NetWeaver Administrator (NWA) page is accessed using the URL:

http://<hostname>:<port>/nwa

My NetWeaver Version is 7.5 and  Java instance number 00, the default NWA HTTP port is 50001.

In my case, the instance number is 00, so I will change the NWA port from 50001 to 8443. After this change, the NWA page will be accessible using port 8443.

Team Requirements

  • The Network Team will open port 8443.
  • AD Team will create One service account. In our case, we are using the SAPD1AUME service account.
  • The Basis Team will perform the LDAP configuration.
  • A Root CA certificate is required. This is a mandatory prerequisite and must be available before starting the configuration.

Preparation Steps

  • First, we need to create a user group. Accordingly, we have created the XX_BASIS_JAVA_ADMINS group.
  • Currently, the NetWeaver Administrator (NWA) URL is accessible via port 50001.

Now add Role to this Group XX_BASIS_JAVA_ADMINS

Administrator  , NWA_SUPERADMIN , SAP_SLD_ADMINISTRATOR

Now go to Configuration--> LDAP Server and fill information about LDAP Server with Service User name and password.

Server Name----->Here you need to provide server hostname.

Server Port-------->636

User------------------>SAPD1AUME

Password------->••••••••

User Path------->OU=Users,DC=,DC=com        <---This information you will get from your LDAP team.

Group Path--------> OU=Users,DC=,DC=com   <---This information you will get from your LDAP team.

Now Validate Configuration.

Now take restart of JAVA Server for change effect.

We have here 2 nodes. Server 1 and server 0

We are here restarting node wise with p r 2 and p r 3 command

After JAVA server restarted again go to User Managemt and check your check your ID

Open User Management--> Configuration-->Open Expert Mode

Check this Parameter ume.logon.allow_cert it should be true

Now go to NWA Configuration and Certificate and Keys

Now Select ICM_SSL_52325_8443. You should have root certificate. We need to import here root certificate

Now import root certificate here.

We can see here root certificate added.

Now Click on Authentication and Single Sign-On:

Select ticket and Add

Add below entry.

 

Now save

Now check 8443 port is enabled or not.

Configuration—SSL

 

Its enabled.

Now, when you open any Java-based URL such as NWA or User Management on port 8443, it will automatically log in using the user ID mapped to the corresponding LDAP group. No password is required for login.

As shown below, the NWA port has been changed from 50001 to 8443.

 

 

Conclusion

Java LDAP communication over port 8443 confirms secure LDAPS usage with encrypted authentication, aligning with security compliance requirements.

entry.title: SAP CAPM Project Lifecycle: From Development to Fiori Preview
entry.content:
Let's see an end  to end demonstration for creating the CAPM project, entity, service with annotations, create the entry via Post man tool and Preview the Application in Fiori.

Below mentioned detailed points covered in this Blog,

  1. BTP Cockpit and its properties
  2. Creation of CAP Project
  3. Linkage of Entity to the HANA DB in the SAP HANA Cloud central
  4. Post man tool Authorization and Post Operation
  5. Preview in the Fiori Application

BTP Cockpit and its properties:

Create a BTP Cock PIT Trial account and Sub Account.

In the Instances and Subscription Add SAP HANA Cloud and SAP Business Application Studio.

Create a Space in the Cloud Foundry, which is a shared, isolated location with an organization (org) used to develop, deploy and manage application and services.

In SAP BTP Cockpit administrators navigate to security->Role collections, create or edit a collection, and add roles-derived from application templates-to define the granular access.

Creation of CAP Project:

In this Business Application studio create a Dev space and create a Project as mentioned below.

 

 

 

Once Project created a project with workspace created. Here I have created ENTITY_CRUD project. As per below image under DB Node we can create the entity and service for the entity create under SRV node.

gen

Auto-generated folder created during build; contains compiled artifacts from your CDS models. You normally don’t edit this manually.

 .vscode

Holds VS Code-specific settings like launch configs and workspace preferences for development.

app

Used for UI/front-end apps (like SAP Fiori); contains HTML, UI5, or other frontend resources.

db

Defines your data model and database artifacts using CDS files (like itemrecordsdb.cds) and deployment configs (undeploy.json).

gen

Another generated folder used during build/deploy; stores intermediate files created by CAP tools.

mta_archives

Contains packaged .mtar files used for deployment to SAP BTP (multi-target application archives).

node_modules

Stores all installed npm dependencies required for your CAP project.

srv

Defines the service layer (business logic & APIs); CDS files like itemrecordssrv.cds expose entities as OData services.

.cdsrc-private.json

Local configuration file for CAP (like profiles, credentials); usually not committed to Git.

.gitignore

Specifies files/folders (like node_modules) that Git should ignore.

 eslint.config.mjs

Configuration for code linting to maintain code quality and standards.

mta.yaml

Defines how the application is structured and deployed in SAP BTP (modules, resources, dependencies).

package.json

Main project config file listing dependencies, scripts, and metadata.

package-lock.json

Locks exact versions of installed dependencies for consistent builds.

 README.md

Documentation file describing the project, setup, and usage.

An entity with Itemrecords created. Here I have used ‘manage, cuid’   from @sap/cds/manage which will create a UUID key for every record creation.

Create a service for itemrecords db which acts as an interface between your data models and outside the world like UI apps or external systems.

Once entity and service generated in the project deploy the project using cf deploy mta_archives/<file>.mtar bash commands. After successful deployment we can see service instances created in the BTP cockpit as shown below.

To link the created entity in BAS to SAP HANA Cloud create a service key for the <entity>-db instance as shown from the below steps.

Choose Entity_CRUD-db and click on the navigation panel where service key need to create. In this illustration I have created as ENTITY_CRUD-db-key.

Once service key generated, we can see the JSON file in the view parameter. This JSON file needs to upload in the SAP HANA explorer to link the DB.

Linkage of Entity to the HANA DB in the SAP HANA Cloud central:

Navigate to Subscribed SAP HANA CLOUD and create instances which allows you to use in-memory computing for real-time transactional and analytical processing. Once instance is created it should be in Running status which indicates Active, available for SQL connections and able to perform data processing, backups and administrative tasks.

Now, open the SAP HANA database Explorer in the SAP HANA cloud central, click on the + icon where instance type to be chosen as HDI container and choose Apply from JSON string where need to paste the JSON file from the service key entity in the BTP cockpit.

 

 

 

 

Once JSON file applied Entity from the BAS linked to the SAP HANA Cloud central.

 

 

 

Post man tool Authorization and Post Operation:

To Authenticate authorization in the Post Man tool go to <entity>-auth in the BTP cockpit as shown below.

 

 

Here I have navigated to the ENTITY_CRUD-auth where service key SK-ENTITY_CRUD is created once service key created a JSON file generated with client ID, client secret, URL.

 

 

 

 

 

 

Client ID, URL client secret mapped to the Authorization in the post man tool and generate the Access Token.

 

 

 

 

Using post operation can create a data to the entity in the CAP project.

 

 

 

Successful created entries showing in the SAP HANA Cloud central.

 

 Preview in the Fiori Application:

To Preview the data in the Fiori application, In the CAP project search with Open Application Generator and create a List Report page.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Once Application created, In the project terminal using Cds watch –profile hybrid can view the preview of the Application in Fiori

Required Annotations can use in the srv which will be reflect in the Fiori Application.

 

Conclusion
This demonstration showcased the seamless integration between SAP Business Application Studio, SAP HANA Cloud, and the Fiori front-end. By leveraging annotations and the CAP service layer, we successfully created a functional CRUD application with automated UUID management and secure API access. You are now ready to extend this project with custom business logic or complex UI enhancements.

entry.title: Re: #CoE API that can be used to query and modify informa...
entry.content:
Hi Jiajia,

An API for the "Additional Credit Limit" is not offered, as this is considered an internal matter. However, the following workarounds are suggested:

  • When obtaining credit limits from outside of Credit Management, directly update the credit limit in the credit segment.
  • To keep this separate, create it as a collateral instead.

Please check notes 3555528 for more info.

Best regards,

Yong

entry.title: Re: Does PRIMARY KEY clustered check stored somewhere
entry.content:
@chris_keating, apparently I need more help.

 

I tried to run the following code:

 

SQLLEN ind[1] = SQL_NTS;

int clusteredId = 0;

ret = SQLBindCol( m_hstmt, 2, SQL_C_SLONG, &clusteredId, 0, &ind1[1] );
if( ret != SQL_SUCCESS && ret != SQL_SUCCESS_WITH_INFO )
{
GetErrorMessage( errorMsg, STMT_ERROR );
result = 1;
}

 

Apparently after executing SQLFetch() the ind[1] is still SQL_NTS despite the fact thar the field is NULL. It should have been SQL_NULL_DATA.

Did I pass wrong parameters in?

 

Thank you.

entry.title: Your guide to SAP Signavio at SAP Sapphire, Orlando 2026!
entry.content:
Calling all process owners and transformation leads!

The countdown to SAP Sapphire 2026 in Orlando has officially begun so we’ve put together a guide for you on everything SAP Signavio happening at the event this year.

In just a few weeks, we will be welcoming you back to Orlando for our annual three-day, in-person flagship event to discover how SAP helps organizations turn potential into performance, through value-driven transformation and process excellence. This event series will also spotlight SAP´s bold new AI vision and reimagined Joule experience.

This year, we will be part of the Business Transformation Management track again and will be presenting our integrated toolchain which encompasses SAP Signavio, SAP LeanIX and WalkMe, combined with Tricentis and Syniti. Over at SAP Signavio, we´ll be focusing on how you can bring direction to ERP transformation and how to deliver impact through unleashing process and AI excellence.

You‘ll find here an easy to navigate breakdown of the key SAP Signavio activities such as keynotes, sessions you don’t want to miss. You will also discover how to talk to our experts, what we´ll be doing at the Experience Center and how to join us virtually. Make sure you stay to the end to hear about some of the extra entertainment we have in store for you!

 

Keynotes

SAP:

- May 12, 8.30-10.00am – The Beginning of Better with Christian Klein SAP, Muhammad Alam SAP, Philipp Herzig SAP, and Sebastian Steinhaeuser SAP

 

 

Business Transformation Management:

- May 12, 10.30-10.50am - Build a transformation capability that delivers measurable value with Sam Masri SAP

 

 

SAP Signavio: 

- May 13, 2.00-2.20pm - Unlocking the New Frontier of Agentic Transformation with SAP Signavio with Dr. Gero Decker SAP

As companies deploy hundreds of AI agents, the real differentiator is no longer automation alone but instead how intelligence is captured and turned into value. Discover how SAP Signavio solutions maximize AI agent impact with the new frontier of process excellence. Learn how to transform collective operational intelligence into sustained impact.

 

Session Highlights

Business Transformation Management:

- May 11, 1.30-1.50pm - Built to Change: How Adaptable Enterprises Win with Oscar Ramos SAP and Jose Cabel SAP

- May 12, 11.30-11.50am - Value as the Common Currency for Transformation and Continuous Improvement with Myrna Acosta SAP

- May 13, 2.30-2.50pm - AI Native... or AI Naive? People and Operations in the Age of Agentic AI with Dee Houchen SAP and David Letteer Hensel Phelps Construction Co

 

SAP Signavio:

- May 12, 10.30-11.15am - Crafting the future: The SAP Signavio solutions 2026 road map with Andre Wenz SAP

- May 12, 1.00-1.20pm - Why high performers focus on process excellence with SAP Signavio solutions with Josèphe Blondaut SAP

- May 12, 1.30-1.50pm - Deliver measurable transformation value confidently with SAP Signavio with Jane Fu SAP and Prasanna Shroti Gilead Sciences Inc

- May 13, 1.30-1.50pm - Unlock AI agent value with SAP Signavio solutions with Noor Naqvi SAP

 

ASUG:

- May 12, 11.30-12.15pm - Why ERP transformations fail: The change management gap with Kara Beck Moog Inc

- May 13, 2.00-2.45pm - Next-gen process management: Process atoms and AI agents from SAP Signavio with Silvio Arcangeli SAP

 

Talk to our experts 🗣️

Join us for 1 on 1 sessions in our expert center, where you can meet our experts who will offer practical guidance helping you with all your seemingly unsolvable problems and questions. Also join us at our demo stations to experience hands-on demos of our latest solutions.

 

SAP Signavio Demo Booths:

- Live showcase: The integrated toolchain for ERP Transformation - BTM2318

- Manage, govern, and get more value from your agentic enterprise with SAP - BTM2319

- Digital twin of the organization: strategic visibility for agentic AI – BTM2320

 

Experience Center ✨

Don´t forget to drop by our interactive showcase of SAP’s latest technologies, industry innovations, and strategic priorities at the Experience Center. This interactive experience will include SAP Signavio solutions and is designed to inspire future innovation and to get you thinking about new possibilities for your business.

 

Don´t Miss Out! 🎾

Celebrity Keynote from Roger Federer: Find your winning edge - May 13, 3:30 p.m.–4:30 p.m.

Join legendary tennis champion and philanthropist Roger Federer for his insights into excelling under pressure. Federer shares how his career-long lessons in versatility, resilience, and strategic thinking can help leaders deliver results no matter the business area.

 

 

 

Party Time 🎉

Live at Disney’s Magic Kingdom® Park: Dave Mathews Band - May 13, 7:30 p.m.–12:00 a.m.

Finish off your Sapphire experience with an unforgettable visit to the iconic attractions, beloved characters and delicious food at Disney’s Magic Kingdom® Park, with a private concert with Dave Mathews Band all against the magical backdrop of Cinderella´s Castle. This is a night of celebration you definitely won’t want to miss out on, and the perfect ending to Sapphire 2026.

 

 

Virtual Sapphire 🖥️

Can´t make it to Orlando? Don´t worry, you can still join us at SAP Sapphire Virtual, May 12-13. The virtual experience will feature live keynotes, daily streaming from the show floor, and ample opportunities to dive deeper into our topics.

Register here.

 

See you there 👋

Sapphire 2026 will be here before you know it so start planning your experience today! If you have any questions about SAP Signavio´s sessions, activities and offerings, feel free to send me an email or contact Jane Fu, Director Product Marketing at SAP Signavio.

entry.title: How to do data purge to a Repository DataBase thats to big
entry.content:
Hello,

We have a Database of the main repository, with a big amount of data, and in the last 6 months grown 110GB.
The table PMLATT is 75% of the database Volume with 410GB, representing 2.6 billions records.

Is there any procedure or action so we can do the purging (Data Purge) of this Database?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How to do data purge to a Repository DataBase thats t...
entry.content:
Hello, 

If you are running a PD version older than v16.7 SP08, please review the resolution section of KBA 3480518 - How to clean up the repository and remove unused data.
Always back up the database before executing the script for safety considerations.

The script described in the article has been implemented in PD 16.7 SP08 and higher versions, so you don’t need to run it manually—it is automatically executed when needed at connection time.

Kind regards

entry.title: The New Look of Success: Navigating the 2026 Experience Updates
entry.content:
We are in the midst of a major evolution for the SuccessFactors experience. Targeting three main areas: the Latest Home Page, the Latest People Profile, and the New Edit/History UI, these changes are designed to make the system feel less like a technical database and more like the modern, intuitive apps we use every day. While these changes may feel daunting, it opens the doors to de-clutter processes and move towards a people-first experience. 

Let’s dive into what’s changing, what we’re excited about, and most importantly, how to prepare for the shift!

The Latest Home Page: Your New Command Center

With the changes coming to the home page, it will feel more than a static landing page, but rather a dynamic launchpad. It is cleaner and more concise, focusing on exactly what needs to be done now.


Some Key Latest Home Page Features:

  • Quick Actions: The time savers of the homepage. Common transactions can be made directly from the homepage in the new quick-actions header, while navigation to specific pages can be done at the click of a button from the quick-links section. There can be up to 14 custom actions and links activated at once, making the day-to-day transactions of a business feel instant.
  • Personal Customization :Employees can now feel a sense of ownership over their digital workspace by having the option to choose which quick actions or quick links they want to show on their home page. If an employee prefers a cleaner look, they can remove their name from the greeting message as well - a small touch to add a bigger, personal feel.
  • Needs Attention Section:All home page cards that require attention are displayed together in one section rather than being broken up, reducing the ‘scroll fatigue’ that came from locating a task notification. These cards can be filtered based by category and type, making navigation a breeze.
  • Organizational Updates: This section of the homepage will retain its standard functionality - providing teams with a space to post about company news & custom content. All of these can be grouped into separate tabs (folders), keeping the page concise while ensuring no important news is missed

Some items to keep in mind:

  • Personalization Guardrails: Quick actions can be made un-hideable by the system admin, ensuring employees don't accidentally hide essential HR tools they actually need
  • Layout Changes: Some buttons/items will now be found in different areas of the homepage. For example, the Team Absence Calendar has shifted from ‘Quick Actions’ over to ‘Quick Links.’
  • Branding Changes: While the traditional banner image has been removed in the latest experience, a new setting ‘Brand Image’ has been added - where the applied image appears on the greeting banner of the homepage
  • Reduced Navigation Option: Quick actions that were already in the main home page drop down (like the Org Chart) have been removed to reduce redundant navigation
  • Filtering of Tasks: The way filters work for tasks in the 'Needs Attention' section actually correspond to how cards were organized in the legacy experience.
  • Custom Tiles: Your old tiles might look a bit different in the new layout. It's a good time to check if the images still fit and if the text used is still relevant
  • Theming: The new home page uses more white space, so your corporate colors might need a slight tweak in your theme(s) to ensure everything flows and fits the companies branding style

Reminder - The Latest Home Page update is currently being turned on in preview environments by default - making now the perfect time to familiarize yourself with your customization options, and get a feel for the flow before it becomes the standard.

The Latest People Profile: Spotlight on Your People

With the update to the people profile, we are moving away from the long-page scrolling view to a sleek, card-based interface. The two distinct areas of updates that are brought in with this new experience are the Profile Preview & Spotlight and the Full Profile. 

 Some Key Spotlight Features:

  • Growth Portfolio Integration: A centralized view of an employee's skills, competencies, and attributes. If enabled, it brings the Talent Intelligence Hub directly into the profile
  • Career Development Integration: Highlights future-focused data like target roles and development goals. This will help target focus towards career growth for managers & their direct reports
  • Organization Information: A simplified header that provides instant context on where the person sits in the hierarchy, including their manager and location, without needing to click into the Org Chart
  • Badges: Employees can see their earned badges instantly, boosting engagement and peer-to-peer appreciation

Some Key Profile Preview Features

  • Quick Actions: Perform some common tasks directly from the spotlight without navigating away from the current page

Some Key Full Profile Features:

  • Categories & Cards: Data is now organized into logical 'Cards' (ex. Personal Information, Payment Information - referred to previously as Portlets) grouped under 'Categories'. This eliminates the infinite scroll layout and allows users to jump directly to the data they need
  • Centralized Permission Control: Admins can now control the visibility of categories & cards within through the 'Configure Latest People Profile' tool. By default, all categories are made visible to everyone or you can choose to specify the permission roles you want to grant access for. From there, the roles can be updated via the 'Permission Roles' tool for more updates, ensuring data privacy is maintained
  • Direct Reports & Team Size View: On a manager profile, you can now view the number of employees who directly report to the manager and the size of the manager's team. This feature is available to be displayed on the Full Profile header.
  • Profile Header Fields: You can now add up to three fields to Full Profile header, allowing you to display additional employee information
  • Actions & Links: Through the actions icon, users will have access to a list of actions & links divided into four groups: Change, View, Take Action, and Links

Some items to keep in mind:

  • Card Organization Blocks: Predefined cards are now locked to specific categories. For example - an employees Payment Information is locked to the Personal Information category of the profile
  • Permission Control: Some fields that were visible in the legacy view may require a quick RBP (Role-Based Permission) check to ensure they appear correctly in the new card-based layout
  • Block Descriptions: The feature to add a block description for custom cards has now been moved to ‘Help Text’, which can be used for all Full Profile cards

The New Edit & History UI: Precision and Transparency

Building on the Full Profile update, the New Edit/History UI replaces the old pop-up windows with streamlined experience. The focus here is on transparency, giving users a clear view of where a record has been and where it is going. This feature is available only for customers who are currently using or are planning to implement Employee Central.

Some Key Features

  • Field Masking: Previously, masked fields were often revealed during the edit process. In the new UI, sensitive values remain masked by default even while editing, ensuring privacy of employee data in all areas
  • Visual Change Tracking: With the previous edit UI, if a field was automatically updated due to onChange rules or associations, there was no clear indicator of this additional change. Now, the changed field will be highlighted in blue, with a pop-up message informing the user that the field was changed based on another field's change
  • Accessible Save Button: Previously, the Save button only enabled when a change was performed. This has been updated to be permanently active, with the system informing you that your record contains no modifications if true upon clicking
  • Consistent Experience: Whether you are updating a phone number, a dependent, or job information, the UI looks and behaves exactly the same way
  • Workflow Clarity: The New Edit UI is fully integrated with Workflows. If a change requires approval, the UI clearly indicates that the "Change is Pending Approval" directly on the card.

Some items to keep in mind:

  • Field Grouping & Logic: With the legacy Configure People Profile admin tool, you were able to organize how fields are displayed on the Editing UI of specific Employee Central blocks. With the new UI, fields will appear in the same order on the Edit UI as they do on the View mode of the portlet, which is based on the data model configuration (BCUI)
  • Field Masking: If you choose to unmask a field while making edits to a record, you won't be able to re-mask it during that specific editing session
  • Shift in Validation Logic: Error messages triggered by onChange rules will now be displayed as warning messages, allowing users to proceed on the UI. To trigger error messages that prevent saving, you will now need to have a distinct onSave rule applied

The Switch: How to Prepare

To ensure a smooth transition to these new experiences, here are a few items to review with your teams for alignment

  • Documentation: Update your internal "How-To" guides. Screenshots of the old pop-up windows will quickly look outdated once the new side-panel UI is enabled
  • Data Accuracy: Since the new UI has stricter validations, it might surface legacy data errors that the old UI ignored. Use this as an opportunity for a "data spring cleaning”
  • Audit Your Permissions (RBP): Since the new profile changes how data is displayed, double-check your Role-Based Permissions to ensure "Confidential" fields stay confidential in the new card views
  • Business Configuration (BCUI) Cleanup: Since the New Edit UI will now display fields following the BCUI layout, take some time to review your Data Model and ensure the most important fields are at the top of each element so users don't have to hunt for them in the new card layout
  • Validate Business Rules: Since onChange rules now behave as warnings in the new UI, now is the time to review your validations. If you have a "must-have" validation that should prevent a user from saving incorrect data, ensure you have a corresponding onSave rule
  • Mobile App Review: The Latest People Profile and Home Page are designed to be mobile-first. If your organization uses the SuccessFactors Mobile App, test these changes on a mobile device. Ensure that your custom quick actions and card layouts translate well to a smaller screen to provide a consistent experience across all platforms
  • Theme Manager Review: The new visual theme introduces more white space and specific card styling. Check your Theme Manager settings to ensure your company’s primary brand colors provide enough contrast against the new card backgrounds. You may find that buttons or links need a slight color tweak to remain accessible and stand out against the cleaner layout

While change can feel like a lot of moving parts, these updates are a large step towards a modern, intuitive HR ecosystem. With these changes, you'll be using a more empowered and efficient way of working within your SuccessFactors Environment.

entry.title: G/L Accounts Mass Change of Company Code Data not possible
entry.content:
Hi SAP experts,

In the “Manage G/L Account Master Data” app, in the company code view, I can no longer perform mass changes as usual. Here is an example where I want to change the field status group:

I select G/L accounts and click the “Mass Change” and “Other Attributes” buttons.

 

But my accounts aren't showing up below.

 

In the chart of accounts view and the controlling area view, it works as usual.

Does anyone know what might have happened? In various other systems I have access to (starter, demo, and implementation systems), it also works in the company code view.

Thanks in advance for any tips!

Best regards,

Rado
